incidents.headline: FSIS alerts E. coli O157:H7 outbreak tied to Kebab Shop beef kofta
incidents.summary: On May 24, 2026, the USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service issued a public health alert after linking beef kofta served at The Kebab Shop restaurant chain to a Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 outbreak. The raw ground beef product was manufactured by Olympia Food Industries, Inc. (Franklin Park, Illinois) on January 6, 2026, and distributed to Kebab Shop locations in California, Texas, and Florida, where 9 confirmed illnesses in California had onset dates ranging from March 27 to April 30, 2026. The Kebab Shop halted all beef kofta sales chain-wide on May 18, 2026; FSIS did not request a formal recall as the product was no longer available for purchase.

secondary_mappings.why: Nine confirmed consumer illnesses and a chain-wide product removal directly harm customer health and service delivery.
FSIS issued a formal public health alert as part of an active outbreak investigation, triggering mandatory regulatory notification obligations.
An active E. coli O157:H7 outbreak associated with named restaurant locations creates material brand and reputational exposure.
Contamination originated with supplier Olympia Food Industries, exposing The Kebab Shop to single-supplier quality failure risk.

incidents.severity_rationale: Nine confirmed human illnesses linked to a deadly pathogen (E. coli O157:H7) with HUS risk, chain-wide product halt across three states, and active federal outbreak investigation; financial impact not yet quantified.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure operational quality controls governing raw ground-beef supplier intake, cold-chain integrity, and cook-temperature verification prevent pathogen contamination from reaching customers at restaurant locations.
Ensure customer-facing communications and menu withdrawal decisions during a live FSIS-linked outbreak event are executed chain-wide within a defined crisis-response window.
Ensure the organisation maintains documented FSIS-compliance posture and can demonstrate supplier audit evidence sufficient to cooperate with or pre-empt a mandatory recall escalation.
Protect brand reputation by ensuring proactive and accurate public disclosure of the scope, timeline, and remediation steps taken in response to the FSIS public health alert.
Maintain stakeholder trust by ensuring consistent messaging to confirmed-illness victims, regulators, media, and franchise/location operators throughout the outbreak response.

Ensure third-party beef supplier (Olympia Food Industries) contractual obligations include HACCP documentation, pathogen-testing attestation, and audit-right provisions enforceable in the event of an FSIS-linked contamination event.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a fast-casual restaurant chain sourcing raw ground beef from a third-party processor, you should immediately audit all supplier lot-traceability records and verify internal cook-temperature logs for any product sharing the Olympia Food Industries supply chain within the next 24–72 hours.

velocity_signal.brief: Recurring STEC O157:H7 vector in fast-casual beef supply chain

incidents.emerging_risk_signal: A growing reliance on small, unaudited third-party processors like Olympia Food Industries to supply specialty ground-beef formats—kofta, koobideh, kebab—across multi-state fast-casual chains creates a systemic STEC O157:H7 exposure that current FSMA traceability requirements do not yet fully resolve.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: The Kebab Shop must verify that incoming ground-beef
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Director of Supply Chain must immediately audit all intake records for ground-beef kofta received from Olympia Food Industries (Franklin Park, IL) and confirm whether lot-level pathogen-testing certificates exist for the January 6, 2026 production run distributed to California, Texas, and Florida locations; any January 2026 production batch lacking a corresponding FSIS-compliant E. coli O157:H7 test certificate must be placed on a documented hold and may not re-enter service without written FSIS clearance. Evidence of completion is a signed intake-audit report listing every Olympia lot received since January 6, 2026, with test-certificate status noted for each, submitted to the VP of Operations within 72 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS public health alert.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation ties the generic incoming-product verification obligation directly to the confirmed January 6, 2026 Olympia production date, the three specific distribution states, and the FSIS alert trigger date, converting an abstract intake-check requirement into a time-bounded, lot-specific audit with a named accountability chain.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement a documented cook-temperature verification protocol for all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Head of Kitchen Operations must, within 5 business days of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert, deploy a written cook-temperature verification protocol to all locations in California, Texas, and Florida requiring that every ground-beef product — including any future kofta formulation — reach and sustain an internal temperature of 160°F (71°C) as measured by a calibrated probe thermometer, with results logged per batch on a standardised temperature-check sheet referencing the FSIS-identified kofta contamination event tied to the January 6, 2026 Olympia production run. Completion is evidenced by signed protocol acknowledgement forms from each location manager and a minimum of 30 consecutive days of temperature logs retained at each site for regulatory inspection.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation anchors the cook-temperature protocol to the specific STEC O157:H7 contamination event and the three states receiving the implicated product, replacing a generic temperature-monitoring directive with a traceable, site-by-site log requirement directly linked to the confirmed outbreak timeline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a root-cause trace-back of the January 6
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Food Safety Officer, in coordination with Olympia Food Industries and a qualified third-party food-safety auditor, must initiate a documented root-cause trace-back of the January 6, 2026 Olympia Franklin Park production run within 10 business days of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert, examining HACCP critical-control-point records (specifically grinding, blending, and cold-chain stages) to determine whether a process failure at that facility was the contamination pathway for the Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 linked to 9 confirmed California illnesses. The trace-back report must identify each CCP evaluated, the finding at each point, and any gap in Olympia's HACCP plan, and must be delivered to The Kebab Shop's VP of Operations and retained for FSIS review.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation moves beyond a general HACCP-audit requirement by specifying the exact production date, facility, and pathogen, and by naming the specific processing stages most likely implicated in ground-beef STEC contamination, giving the investigative team a defined scope rather than an open-ended mandate.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update The Kebab Shop's supplier qualification criteria to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Procurement Director must revise the supplier qualification standard for all FSIS-regulated ground-beef manufacturers within 30 days of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert to require, as a condition of acceptance at any California, Texas, or Florida distribution point, the submission of a lot-specific E. coli O157:H7 test certificate — issued by an accredited third-party laboratory — accompanying each delivery; this requirement applies retroactively to any Olympia Food Industries lot presented for acceptance after the May 18, 2026 chain-wide kofta halt and prospectively to all future ground-beef suppliers. The revised standard must be approved by the VP of Operations, incorporated into the supplier onboarding agreement, and countersigned by each active ground-beef supplier within 60 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation responds specifically to the confirmed gap that allowed an untested January 6, 2026 Olympia production run to reach three states without lot-level pathogen clearance, converting the generic supplier-qualification update into a mandatory pre-acceptance certificate regime with retroactive and prospective scope.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Execute a chain-wide product withdrawal audit confirming that
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Director of Operations must, within 48 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS public health alert, obtain and consolidate signed inventory-reconciliation forms from every California, Texas, and Florida location confirming that all beef kofta produced by Olympia Food Industries on or after January 6, 2026 has been physically removed from inventory, with each unit accounted for as either destroyed (with a signed destruction log) or quarantined pending disposition instructions; any location that cannot produce a complete reconciliation must escalate immediately to the VP of Operations and the designated FSIS regulatory liaison. Completed destruction and quarantine records must be retained for a minimum of three years and made available to FSIS upon request.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation transforms a generic product-withdrawal audit into a location-specific, deadline-driven reconciliation exercise anchored to the confirmed January 6, 2026 production date and the three confirmed distribution states, with explicit escalation triggers for sites that cannot close out their inventory.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Notify all California Kebab Shop customers who purchased
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Chief Marketing Officer must, within 24 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS public health alert, extract from the loyalty-programme and digital-order platforms (app, online ordering) all customer records reflecting a beef kofta purchase at any California location between March 27 and April 30, 2026 — the confirmed illness-onset window — and send each identified customer a direct notification via their registered contact channel (email, push notification, or SMS) referencing the FSIS May 24, 2026 alert, advising them to seek medical attention if they experienced symptoms consistent with E. coli O157:H7 infection, and directing them to the dedicated consumer-response hotline. A record of all notification attempts, delivery confirmations, and undeliverable contacts must be retained and provided to the FSIS regulatory liaison and the California Department of Public Health within 5 business days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation operationalises the customer-notification obligation by specifying the exact purchase-date window tied to the confirmed California illness-onset dates, naming the digital channels available to The Kebab Shop, and requiring delivery-confirmation records that serve both public-health reporting and litigation-preparation purposes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a dedicated consumer-response hotline within 48h of
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's VP of Operations must stand up a dedicated consumer-response hotline no later than 48 hours after the May 24, 2026 FSIS public health alert, staffed by personnel trained to collect caller symptom onset dates, purchase locations, and contact information, and to triage callers reporting symptoms consistent with E. coli O157:H7 (bloody diarrhoea, severe abdominal cramps, onset within 3–8 days of beef kofta consumption) to the California Department of Public Health (CDPH) reporting pathway; all call records, including caller identity, symptom description, and triage outcome, must be logged in a case-management system and preserved for use in regulatory reporting and litigation preparation. The hotline number must appear on The Kebab Shop's website homepage, the CEO's public statement, and all customer notification messages sent pursuant to AC-OPS-032-ECL-01.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation links the hotline requirement directly to the STEC O157:H7 symptom profile and the CDPH reporting pathway relevant to the 9 confirmed California cases, ensuring that the response infrastructure is calibrated to the specific pathogen and jurisdiction rather than serving as a generic customer-service channel.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Designate a single regulatory liaison authorised to respond
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's General Counsel must, within 24 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS public health alert, formally designate a single named regulatory liaison — with written authority to bind the company on factual representations — who is responsible for receiving and responding to all inquiries from FSIS and the California Department of Public Health arising from the confirmed E. coli O157:H7 outbreak linked to Olympia Food Industries beef kofta; this liaison must acknowledge any agency inquiry within 4 hours of receipt and must maintain a timestamped log of every agency communication, action taken, and commitment made. The designation and contact information must be communicated to FSIS and CDPH in writing by the end of the first business day following the alert.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation names the two specific agencies involved in this outbreak — FSIS at the federal level and CDPH given the 9 confirmed California cases — and establishes a 4-hour SLA with a timestamped communication log, converting an abstract liaison-designation requirement into an immediately auditable accountability structure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Preserve and produce to FSIS all supplier intake records
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Director of Supply Chain and Director of Operations must jointly compile and preserve, within 72 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert, a complete records package covering all Olympia Food Industries beef kofta received at any California, Texas, or Florida location from January 6, 2026 (confirmed production date) through May 18, 2026 (chain-wide halt), including purchase orders, delivery receipts, cold-chain temperature logs, receiving-inspection records, and any internal customer-complaint or illness-report data; all records must be placed on a litigation and regulatory hold, access restricted to authorised personnel, and a master index delivered to the designated FSIS regulatory liaison and General Counsel within the same 72-hour window. Physical and digital records must be retained for a minimum of five years or the duration of any regulatory or civil action, whichever is longer.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation specifies the exact date range and record types relevant to the confirmed distribution and illness-onset timeline, and imposes a litigation-hold alongside the regulatory-production obligation, reflecting the concurrent exposure from 9 confirmed illnesses and the ongoing FSIS inquiry.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess whether the 6-day gap between The Kebab
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's General Counsel must, within 48 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert, retain outside California food-safety regulatory counsel to assess whether the 6-day gap between The Kebab Shop's internal beef kofta halt on May 18, 2026 and the FSIS public health alert on May 24, 2026 triggers mandatory disclosure obligations under California Health and Safety Code retail food-safety provisions or FSMA Section 204 enhanced traceability requirements, and must provide a written legal memorandum to the CEO and VP of Operations documenting the conclusion and any required remedial disclosures. If counsel identifies a mandatory disclosure obligation, the disclosure must be made to the relevant authority within 24 hours of that determination, with a copy retained in the regulatory-liaison communication log.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation converts a generic disclosure-assessment requirement into a time-bounded legal analysis focused on the specific 6-day internal-halt-to-public-alert gap, naming the California and FSMA regulatory frameworks most likely to apply given the confirmed distribution states and illness jurisdiction.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Issue a public statement from The Kebab Shop's
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's CEO must issue a public statement within 24 hours of the May 24, 2026 FSIS public health alert that: (a) acknowledges the FSIS alert by name and date; (b) confirms that The Kebab Shop halted all beef kofta sales chain-wide on May 18, 2026; (c) states that the implicated product was manufactured by Olympia Food Industries in Franklin Park, Illinois on January 6, 2026; (d) describes the corrective actions taken with Olympia Food Industries, including the suspension of the supply relationship and initiation of a root-cause investigation; and (e) provides the consumer-response hotline number for customers who purchased beef kofta at California, Texas, or Florida locations. The statement must be published on The Kebab Shop's website, distributed via press release to major wire services, and posted to all official social-media accounts, with a copy retained for the regulatory-liaison file.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation structures the CEO statement around the specific confirmed facts of this outbreak — the Olympia production date, the three distribution states, the May 18 halt — rather than leaving the content of the public acknowledgement undefined, ensuring the statement is legally accurate relative to FSIS-confirmed information and operationally useful to affected consumers.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Monitor and respond to social-media and review-platform mentions
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's Head of Communications must activate social-media monitoring across Twitter/X, Instagram, Facebook, Google Reviews, and Yelp for the keyword combinations 'Kebab Shop + E. coli', 'Kebab Shop + kofta', 'Kebab Shop + sick', and related variants within 2 hours of the FSIS May 24, 2026 alert publication, with an assigned team member reviewing alerts in real time and responding to public posts in California, Texas, and Florida markets within 2 hours of each post's appearance using a pre-approved response template referencing the CEO statement and consumer-response hotline. All monitored posts, The Kebab Shop's responses, and the sentiment trajectory must be logged daily and reported to the VP of Communications and General Counsel for the first 30 days following the alert.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation specifies the exact platforms, keyword strings relevant to this outbreak, and the three specific state markets where reputational amplification risk is highest given confirmed distribution and illness concentration, replacing a generic social-media monitoring directive with an operationally actionable real-time response workflow.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Invoke audit-right clauses in The Kebab Shop's supply
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's General Counsel must, within 5 business days of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert, formally invoke the audit-right clause in The Kebab Shop's supply agreement with Olympia Food Industries via written notice, demanding production of all HACCP plan documents, CCP monitoring records, sanitation logs, corrective-action records, and USDA inspection and non-conformance reports for the Franklin Park, Illinois facility covering October 1, 2025 through March 31, 2026 — the quarter immediately preceding and encompassing the confirmed January 6, 2026 production run. Olympia Food Industries must be required to respond within 10 business days, and any refusal or partial production must be escalated immediately to FSIS and documented in the regulatory-liaison log as potential grounds for supply-contract termination.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation grounds the audit-right invocation in the specific contractual mechanism and defines the document scope by reference to the confirmed production date and the Q4 2025–Q1 2026 window most likely to reveal antecedent HACCP failures, converting a generic audit-right clause into a demand letter with defined deliverables and an escalation consequence.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Require Olympia Food Industries to provide a corrective-action
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's VP of Operations must formally notify Olympia Food Industries in writing within 5 business days of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert that continued supply eligibility is conditioned on Olympia's delivery, within 30 calendar days, of a written corrective-action plan (CAP) specifically addressing the contamination pathway identified in the January 6, 2026 Franklin Park production run of ground-beef kofta; the CAP must include a root-cause analysis, the specific HACCP modifications implemented, corrective sanitation procedures, and a third-party verification schedule, and must be reviewed and approved by The Kebab Shop's Food Safety Officer before any Olympia product is accepted at any California, Texas, or Florida location. Failure to deliver an approvable CAP within 30 days triggers automatic suspension of the supply relationship pending a formal re-qualification audit.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation makes the 30-day CAP requirement a contractually enforced condition of continued supply rather than a goodwill request, ties it directly to the confirmed January 6 production run and Franklin Park facility, and defines the CAP content elements required to address the specific STEC O157:H7 contamination risk profile.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Add an FSIS-alert trigger clause to all future
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Kebab Shop's General Counsel must ensure that all new or renewed ground-beef supply contracts — beginning with any agreement executed with Olympia Food Industries or any replacement supplier following this incident — include a mandatory FSIS-alert trigger clause requiring the supplier to: (a) place any implicated production lot on an automatic hold within 24 hours of receiving or becoming aware of any FSIS inquiry, consumer illness report, or internal non-conformance linked to a delivered lot; and (b) provide written notification to The Kebab Shop's designated regulatory liaison within the same 24-hour window, specifying the lot number, production date, and distribution points affected. This clause must include a liquidated-damages provision for non-compliance and must be incorporated into the standard supplier agreement template reviewed and approved by the VP of Operations and General Counsel within 45 days of the May 24, 2026 FSIS alert.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation responds directly to the confirmed sequence in which The Kebab Shop halted sales on May 18 but the FSIS public alert did not follow until May 24, by building a contractual early-warning obligation into future supply agreements that would require supplier-side notification before a regulatory alert reaches the public, closing the information-gap that characterised this incident.

incidents.headline: Electrolux Italy plants hit by 8-hour worker strike on May 25
incidents.summary: On May 25, 2026, workers at Electrolux manufacturing plants across Italy staged an 8-hour strike action, with a national rally held at the Ministry of Enterprises in Rome. The stoppage is part of a wider pattern of industrial unrest in Italy's manufacturing sector, with union demands centred on job security and terms and conditions. The action disrupted production across multiple Electrolux sites in Italy, a country where the company has historically maintained significant appliance manufacturing capacity.

secondary_mappings.why: The strike is a direct labour relations event driven by union demands over employment conditions at Italian plants.
An 8-hour production stoppage at multiple sites tests business continuity and recovery of manufacturing output.
Recurring Italian plant industrial action reflects unresolved strategic questions about Electrolux's European manufacturing footprint and restructuring programme.

incidents.severity_rationale: Single-day 8-hour stoppage at Italian plants; disruption is measurable but time-limited, with no confirmed financial disclosure and no safety risk.

sources.title: Current Strikes in Italy (May 2026) — Strike Tracker

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure continuity of manufacturing operations across Electrolux's five Italian plants during the 25 May 2026 strike and any successor industrial actions.
Maintain minimum viable output at Porcia, Susegana, Forlì, and Solaro facilities sufficient to fulfil committed retailer replenishment cycles through Q2 2026.
Protect Electrolux Italy's production scheduling from cascading disruption if the 25 May strike is followed by further national or sector-wide stoppages in late May 2026.
Manage the workforce impact of the announced 1,700-position optimisation in compliance with Italian collective-redundancy procedures and CIGS entitlements.
Activate and validate business-continuity provisions for the Cerreto d'Esi closure and partial production halts at Forlì and Porcia.
Align Electrolux's Italian restructuring timeline with the strategic review commitments made to MIMIT at the 25 May Palazzo Piacentini roundtable.

Electrolux cited the critical situation in the European market, rising production costs, and Asian competition as reasons

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate production-continuity protocols for all five Italian plants
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By close of business 25 May 2026, the Italian Operations Director must activate documented production-continuity protocols at all five Italian plants — Porcia, Susegana, Forlì, Solaro, and Cerreto d'Esi — with written confirmation from each site manager that the Porcia washer-dryer and Susegana 'Genesi' refrigerator lines have resumed or are scheduled to resume at full rated capacity. The protocol activation must include a triage decision on whether partial-shift extensions or weekend overtime at Porcia and Susegana are authorised to offset the units lost during the 8-hour stoppage of 25 May 2026. Evidence of completion is a signed site-readiness report from each plant manager, countersigned by the Italian Operations Director, lodged in the incident management system by 23:59 CET on 25 May 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control mandates continuity activation; this adaptive control names the specific lines (Porcia washer-dryer, Susegana 'Genesi') at highest structural risk given the announced restructuring, imposes a same-day deadline tied to the confirmed 8-hour stoppage, and specifies the physical evidence chain required to confirm execution rather than intent.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a same-day output-loss audit for 25 May across Porcia
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Italian Supply Chain Controller must complete, by 09:00 CET on 26 May 2026, a quantified output-loss audit covering each of the five affected Italian plants — Porcia, Susegana, Forlì, Solaro, and Cerreto d'Esi — expressing lost units as a percentage shortfall against each site's confirmed Q2 2026 European retailer delivery schedule, disaggregated by product line and named retail customer where contractual delivery windows are at risk. The audit must flag any retailer commitment where the 25 May stoppage alone — without further industrial action — causes a breach of agreed lead times, and must assign a named account manager to each at-risk commitment for immediate customer communication. The completed audit, in the approved incident-reporting template, is the mandatory input to the 26 May executive briefing required under AC-OPS-009-RIF-01.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for an output-loss audit; this adaptive control anchors it to the specific five named sites, requires disaggregation to named retailer commitments against Q2 2026 schedules, and creates a hard handoff to the executive briefing control, preventing the audit from being completed but not acted upon.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Resequence the Italian production schedule within 72 hours
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 28 May 2026 (72 hours after the confirmed 25 May stoppage), the Forlì Plant Manager, in coordination with the Italian Supply Chain Controller, must produce a revised production schedule for the Forlì site that quantifies the recovery plan for hob output lost on 25 May and demonstrates how the shortfall will be recovered within the remainder of Q2 2026, given that hob production represents approximately one-third of Forlì's reported revenue. The revised schedule must identify any raw-material or component pre-positioning required to support accelerated hob runs, confirm available machine capacity and shift patterns, and be approved by the Italian Operations Director before submission. If full in-period recovery is not achievable at Forlì alone, the schedule must include a named alternative site or subcontract option with a cost delta.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses schedule resequencing; this adaptive control locks the 72-hour deadline to the confirmed 25 May date, centres the obligation on the hob line's disproportionate revenue significance at Forlì specifically, and requires either a complete in-period recovery plan or a costed alternative — preventing a plan that defers risk without resolving it.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement a communications protocol with FIM-CISL
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 17:00 CET on 27 May 2026 (48 hours after the confirmed 25 May strike), Electrolux Italy's Head of Industrial Relations must issue written meeting requests to the national secretariats of FIM-CISL, FIOM-CGIL, and UILM-UIL proposing a structured bilateral calendar covering at minimum: (a) a first plenary session no later than 6 June 2026, (b) site-level consultation sessions at Porcia, Susegana, Forlì, Solaro, and Cerreto d'Esi within the Legge 223 consultation window, and (c) a pre-agreed agenda framework that separates Cerreto d'Esi closure terms from the workforce-reduction consultations at the remaining four sites. Evidence of completion is confirmed receipt of the written requests by all three union secretariats, logged with timestamps, and a draft calendar circulated internally for management approval.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a communications protocol; this adaptive control names all three confirmed unions, imposes a 48-hour deadline anchored to 25 May, and separates the Cerreto d'Esi closure track (confirmed for full shutdown) from the partial-reduction track, reflecting the materially different negotiating dynamics at each and preventing a single undifferentiated process that could collapse under competing union priorities.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Stress-test logistics and distribution capacity at Susegana and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 May 2026, the European Logistics Director must complete a written stress-test of the Susegana and Porcia outbound distribution networks modelling the scenario in which the confirmed 25 May 8-hour stoppage escalates to a rolling 24-hour action commencing in June 2026, with the model required to quantify: (a) the maximum number of days before Scandinavian retailer replenishment lanes (principally for the 'Genesi' refrigerator line from Susegana) breach contracted service levels, (b) the equivalent threshold for Central European retailer replenishment lanes dependent on Porcia washer-dryer output, and (c) the minimum buffer-stock positions at Susegana and Porcia bonded warehouses needed to sustain retailer service levels through a 10-day rolling action. The stress-test must be signed off by the Italian Operations Director and forwarded to the Group CFO as a financial-exposure input.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires logistics stress-testing; this adaptive control anchors the scenario to the confirmed 8-hour stoppage as the baseline, names the specific geographic retailer lanes (Scandinavian, Central European), names the specific product lines at each site, and requires explicit buffer-stock thresholds rather than qualitative risk commentary.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Prepare the collective-redundancy notification dossier for the 1
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Italian HR Director and external Italian labour counsel must, by 30 May 2026, file or confirm readiness to file the collective-redundancy notification dossier with the relevant territorial Labour Offices (DTL) under Legislative Decree 223/1991 (Legge 223), treating 11 May 2026 (the confirmed public announcement of approximately 1,700 redundancies across an Italian workforce of approximately 4,500) as the statutory trigger date for the 75-day consultation clock. The dossier must be disaggregated by site — covering Porcia, Susegana, Forlì, Solaro, and Cerreto d'Esi — with Cerreto d'Esi documented separately as a full-closure notification given its confirmed status, and must include the criteria for worker selection, proposed social measures, and the timeline for phased exits aligned to the CIGS pre-authorisation application under AC-PPL-027-RIF-01. Completion evidence is the DTL's acknowledgement receipt for each site-level notification.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a Legge 223 dossier; this adaptive control fixes the statutory clock to the confirmed 11 May announcement date, mandates site-level disaggregation reflecting the confirmed multi-site nature of the redundancies, and separates the Cerreto d'Esi full-closure notification as a legally distinct filing obligation — preventing a single composite dossier that obscures the closure track and risks DTL rejection.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Engage CIGS (Cassa Integrazione Guadagni Straordinaria) pre-authorisation for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 May 2026, the Italian HR Director must submit CIGS (Cassa Integrazione Guadagni Straordinaria) pre-authorisation applications to INPS for the workers at Cerreto d'Esi (approximately 170 confirmed employees facing closure), Forlì, and Porcia, citing 'crisi aziendale' as the statutory ground and referencing the confirmed 1,700-position restructuring announced on 11 May 2026 as the supporting industrial context. Each application must specify the anticipated duration of income-support coverage aligned to the 75-day Legge 223 consultation window and the projected closure/reduction timeline, and must be accompanied by the site-level workforce data extracted from the Legge 223 dossier prepared under AC-PPL-026-RIF-01. The Italian HR Director is accountable for INPS submission confirmation receipts for all three sites, to be logged in the incident management system by end of business 30 May 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires CIGS pre-authorisation; this adaptive control names the three specific sites confirmed or reported as facing the most acute workforce impact, fixes the 30 May 2026 deadline, anchors the statutory ground to the confirmed restructuring, and creates an explicit dependency on the Legge 223 dossier to ensure the INPS application is evidentiary-complete rather than submitted with gaps that could delay INPS authorisation and leave workers without income protection.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Validate that business-continuity plans for the Cerreto d'Esi
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 27 May 2026, the Italian Operations Director must validate in writing that the existing business-continuity plan for Cerreto d'Esi explicitly addresses: (a) the transfer or discontinuation of the range-hood product line manufactured at the Marche facility, naming the receiving site or external manufacturer and the transition timeline, and (b) the reallocation or redeployment plan for the work orders currently assigned to the approximately 170 Cerreto d'Esi workers, with any open customer orders redirected to a named alternative production site with confirmed capacity. If the existing BCP does not address either element, the Italian Operations Director must commission an updated plan within five business days, with interim manual work-order redirection put in place immediately. Evidence of completion is a signed BCP validation checklist countersigned by the Italian Operations Director and the Group Supply Chain Director.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires BCP validation for the closure; this adaptive control names the specific product line at risk (range hoods), the specific confirmed workforce headcount (approximately 170), and requires the BCP to resolve both the product-line transfer and the open-work-order redistribution as discrete executable items — preventing a BCP that confirms closure planning in principle but leaves product-line and order continuity unresolved.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Brief Electrolux AB's Group CEO and CFO by
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 17:00 CET on 27 May 2026, the Italian CFO must deliver to Electrolux AB's Group CEO and Group CFO a written financial-exposure briefing quantifying: (a) the direct EBIT impact of units lost across all five Italian sites on 25 May 2026 against Q2 2026 European Home Appliances segment guidance, incorporating the output-loss audit produced under AC-OPS-007-RIF-01; (b) the restructuring charge estimate associated with the confirmed 1,700-position reduction and Cerreto d'Esi closure, disaggregated between cash and non-cash items; and (c) a sensitivity table showing the incremental EBIT impact if the 25 May 8-hour stoppage escalates to a rolling action in June 2026, using the logistics stress-test inputs from AC-OPS-022-RIF-01. The briefing must include a clear statement of whether existing Q2 2026 EBIT guidance for the European Home Appliances segment remains defensible and, if not, the indicative revision range.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires an executive financial briefing; this adaptive control names the specific segment (European Home Appliances), mandates integration of outputs from two other adaptive controls to prevent duplicate or inconsistent figures reaching the Group CEO, and requires an explicit guidance-defensibility statement — the decision that Group leadership actually needs to make before any external disclosure obligation is triggered.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Formalise Electrolux's response position for the MIMIT Palazzo
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Before the next government-convened MIMIT session following the confirmed 25 May 2026 Palazzo Piacentini roundtable (chaired by Industry Minister Adolfo Urso), Electrolux Italy's Managing Director must obtain internal Board approval for a defined concession envelope covering: (a) the maximum additional investment in re-skilling and outplacement for the approximately 1,700 affected workers that Electrolux is prepared to commit publicly, expressed as a euro figure; (b) any site-preservation measures — such as partial conversion or reduced-closure scope at Cerreto d'Esi or volume rebalancing at Forlì — that management is authorised to propose to MIMIT without further Board referral; and (c) the conditions under which Electrolux would accept a government-mediated pause in the Legge 223 consultation clock. The approved concession envelope must be documented in a Board resolution or delegated authority memorandum held by Italian legal counsel before the Managing Director enters the next ministerial session.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires formalising a response position for the MIMIT roundtable; this adaptive control names the specific minister and venue confirmed in the facts, requires the concession envelope to be pre-approved at Board level with a euro-denominated investment ceiling rather than left to negotiating discretion, and includes the legally significant question of whether Electrolux would accept a consultation-clock pause — a concession that, if offered without authority, could create an unintended legal commitment under Legge 223.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Scenario-plan a strategic alternative to the announced 1
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 24 June 2026 (30 days after the confirmed 25 May MIMIT roundtable), Electrolux Italy's Strategy Director, working with the Group Head of Public Affairs, must present to MIMIT a documented strategic alternative to the announced 1,700-position reduction, comprising at minimum one fully costed option from the following: (a) partial conversion of one or more of the five Italian sites to a higher-margin or adjacent product category, with a named product category, indicative capex requirement, and projected headcount retention figure; or (b) a formal application or eligibility assessment for EU Just Transition Fund financing under the Italian JFTF territorial plan, identifying the specific fund window, the eligible sites (prioritising Cerreto d'Esi given its confirmed closure status and Marche regional eligibility), and the timeline to a funding decision. The presentation must be reviewed by Italian labour counsel to confirm it does not inadvertently restart the Legge 223 statutory clock, and must be submitted to MIMIT in writing with a covering letter from the Group CEO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires scenario-planning a strategic alternative for MIMIT; this adaptive control fixes the 30-day deadline to the confirmed 25 May roundtable date, requires a costed rather than conceptual alternative, names the EU Just Transition Fund as the specific financing mechanism to evaluate, and mandates a Legge 223 legal review — because presenting a substantive restructuring alternative without that review could trigger an unintended statutory restart with material consequences for the redundancy timeline.

incidents.headline: Italy national freight transport 120-hour stoppage begins May 25
incidents.summary: Starting at 12:01 AM on May 25, 2026, a 120-hour national halt of road haulage services began across Italy, running through midnight on May 29, promoted by major transport trade associations including Cna Fita, Confartigianato Trasporti, and Fai. The action, driven by rising operating costs particularly fuel prices, threatens widespread disruption to logistics and goods distribution across the country during a five-day window. Regulators noted the strike watchdog has assessed the action's scope, and with Italy's broader May strike calendar already strained, the compounding impact on supply chains is expected to be significant.
incidents.entity: Italian road haulage sector (Cna Fita / Confartigianato Trasporti)

secondary_mappings.why: The stoppage is a coordinated labour action by multiple haulage trade unions and associations over pay and fuel cost grievances.
A 5-day nationwide freight halt materially threatens the business continuity of manufacturers, retailers, and distributors reliant on Italian road logistics.
End-customer delivery commitments across Italy will be disrupted for the full 120-hour duration.
Rising fuel costs linked to Strait of Hormuz disruption and broader geopolitical instability are cited as the structural driver of the action.

incidents.severity_rationale: A 5-day national road freight halt in Italy will cause cascading supply chain disruption across manufacturing, retail, and distribution; magnitude elevated by concurrent Italian strike calendar and Middle East logistics pressures.

graded_claims.claim: A 120-hour national halt of Italian road haulage services began at 00:01 on 25 May 2026 and runs through midnight on 29 May 2026.

The action is promoted by Cna Fita, Confartigianato Trasporti, Fai, Fiap, Sna/Casartigiani, Unitai, Agci, Legacoop Produzione e Servizi, and Confcooperative Lavoro e Servizi under the UNATRAS umbrella.
The mobilisation is driven by rising operating costs, particularly fuel prices and unpaid diesel excise duty relief for the period March 19 to May 22, 2026.
UNATRAS confirmed the decision at an extraordinary leadership meeting on 7 May 2026 and says the strike will be called off only if the Italian government responds immediately to sector demands.
UNATRAS is demanding a temporary suspension of contribution and insurance payment obligations and full recovery of unpaid diesel excise duty.
The stoppage could choke ports in Genoa, Livorno, and Gioia Tauro during the five-day window.
Italy's broader May 2026 strike calendar also includes rail stoppages on 28-29 May and a national general strike on 29 May, compounding the logistics impact.

Italy's broader May 2026 strike calendar also includes rail stoppages on 28-29 May and a national general strike on 29 May, compounding the logistics impact.

held_claims.claim: The stoppage could choke ports in Genoa, Livorno, and Gioia Tauro during the five-day window.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a supply chain with Italian road-dependent inbound or outbound freight legs, you should immediately invoke your transport-disruption BCP, pre-position 5-day safety stock at Italian distribution centres, notify customers of delay risk, and activate intermodal rail alternatives via Verona or Bologna yards before 25 May 12:01.

velocity_signal.pattern: Repeat Italian road haulage industrial action driven by fuel-cost grievances
velocity_signal.brief: Second Italian national haulage halt in 5 weeks

incidents.emerging_risk_signal: If the Italian government does not concede diesel excise relief by 29 May 2026, UNATRAS may escalate to indefinite rotating stoppages, creating a structural rather than episodic Italian logistics risk through Q3 2026.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate the freight-mode contingency protocol covering rail intermodal
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Effective 24 May 2026 no later than 22:00 CET, activate the freight-mode contingency protocol substituting Italian road haulage capacity with rail intermodal transfers at Verona Quadrante Europa, Bologna Interporto, and Milan Smistamento yards for the confirmed 120-hour UNATRAS stoppage window (25 May 00:01 through 29 May 24:00). The Head of Intermodal Operations must confirm slot bookings on RFI-operated services through each of the three yards by 23:30 on 24 May and log confirmed wagon/unit allocations in the central transport management system as evidence of activation. Any yard where intermodal capacity is unavailable or oversubscribed must be escalated to the Senior Logistics Director within one hour of confirmation failure, with an alternative slot at the nearest viable interchange identified and booked before 00:01 on 25 May.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control specifies rail intermodal substitution in principle; this adaptation names the three specific RFI interchange yards, sets a hard pre-stoppage confirmation deadline of 23:30 on 24 May, and mandates a documented escalation path for capacity shortfall — elements absent from the generic directive that are critical given the abrupt onset at 00:01 on 25 May.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Trigger safety-stock drawdown authorisation for all Italian distribution centres
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 18:00 CET on 24 May 2026, the Italian Distribution Network Manager must issue a formal safety-stock drawdown authorisation to all Italian distribution centre site managers, directing each site to verify and document forward inventory cover of at least five days for all ambient and chilled SKUs against average daily dispatch volumes for the preceding 30-day period. Each site manager must submit a completed stock-cover confirmation form — recording SKU-level coverage days and any lines falling below the five-day threshold — to the central logistics operations inbox by 22:00 on 24 May; lines below threshold must trigger an emergency replenishment purchase order raised before 00:01 on 25 May or a documented explanation why replenishment is not achievable. The Distribution Network Manager is the accountable owner; evidence of completion is the timestamped confirmation forms and any associated purchase orders visible in the ERP system.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control authorises safety-stock drawdown in principle; this adaptation fixes the authorisation deadline at 18:00 on 24 May, imposes a per-SKU five-day cover verification with site-level documentation, and requires same-night emergency purchase orders for below-threshold lines — converting a standing permission into a time-bounded executable sequence tied to the UNATRAS stoppage onset.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Issue formal force-majeure or delay notifications to Italian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Commercial Contracts must issue written force-majeure or delay notifications to all Italian shipper counterparties with active contracts covering road delivery legs by no later than 06:00 CET on 25 May 2026, citing the UNATRAS 120-hour national road haulage action confirmed by Cna Fita, Confartigianato Trasporti, and the full UNATRAS coalition as the triggering event for the period 25 May 00:01 to 29 May 24:00. Each notification must reference the applicable contractual force-majeure clause by number, state that the company reserves all rights to suspend penalty-clause obligations for the duration, and include the name and contact details of the designated contract escalation manager. Evidence of completion is a timestamped email dispatch log showing all counterparties notified before 06:00, retained in the contract management system against each counterparty record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires force-majeure notification before the stoppage commences; this adaptation specifies the exact notification deadline of 06:00 on 25 May, mandates that each notice cite the UNATRAS coalition and the confirmed 25–29 May window by name, and requires clause-level contractual referencing — ensuring the notices are legally substantive rather than generic advisories.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Invoke the business-continuity plan's transport-disruption annex specifically referencing
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 23:00 CET on 24 May 2026, the Business Continuity Lead must formally invoke the transport-disruption annex of the company business-continuity plan, recording the UNATRAS May 25–29 2026 120-hour Italian national road haulage stoppage as the named trigger event in the BCP activation log. Structured 72-hour escalation checkpoints are required at 00:01 on 28 May and at close of business on 29 May, with each checkpoint producing a written situation report from the Logistics Continuity Team to the Senior Logistics Director covering: percentage of planned Italian road volumes re-routed, current safety-stock cover days at each distribution centre, and any SLA breaches logged since the preceding checkpoint. Evidence of completion is the dated BCP activation log entry and the two checkpoint situation reports filed in the incident management system.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control invokes the BCP transport annex in general terms; this adaptation names the UNATRAS stoppage as the explicit trigger, anchors the two 72-hour checkpoints to calendar dates (28 May and 29 May) within the confirmed stoppage window, and specifies the three mandatory data points each checkpoint report must contain — making the escalation cadence auditable rather than aspirational.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Re-route perishable and time-critical loads through Italian rail
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 22:00 CET on 24 May 2026, the Perishable and Time-Critical Freight Manager must confirm that all loads classified as perishable or time-critical with Italian delivery legs scheduled for 25–29 May have been re-routed to either: (a) RFI rail freight services operating on the Italian national network, or (b) cross-border road corridors via Switzerland (Gotthard/San Bernardino) or Austria (Brenner Pass), with carrier bookings confirmed and documented. Any load for which re-routing confirmation cannot be obtained before 00:01 on 25 May must be escalated immediately to the Senior Logistics Director with a written rationale and a proposed disposition (deferral, alternative mode, or customer notification). Evidence of completion is the re-routing confirmation log in the TMS showing carrier, mode, corridor, and booking reference for each affected load, with all entries timestamped before 00:01 on 25 May.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control directs re-routing of perishables to rail or cross-border road in principle; this adaptation names the specific RFI network and the Switzerland and Austria road corridors, sets a hard 22:00 on 24 May confirmation deadline, and requires load-level booking records in the TMS — converting a routing preference into a per-load verified execution step.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain real-time shipment visibility across all active intermodal
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: From 00:01 on 25 May through 24:00 on 29 May 2026, the Central Logistics Control Tower must maintain real-time shipment visibility across all active intermodal substitutions — including RFI rail services and cross-border Switzerland/Austria road movements — by ingesting carrier-telematics and wagon-tracking feeds at a maximum four-hour interval, with each feed update logged against the shipment record in the TMS. At each four-hour interval the Control Tower duty officer must produce a status snapshot covering: number of shipments in transit, number delayed beyond planned ETA by more than four hours, and any corridor or interchange (Verona, Bologna, Milan Smistamento) showing systemic congestion; snapshots must be escalated to the Senior Logistics Director by automated dashboard alert. Evidence of completion is the continuous TMS feed log and the dated escalation records retained in the incident management system for the full five-day window.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires real-time visibility and four-hour escalation during an intermodal substitution; this adaptation specifies the exact intermodal corridors and interchange nodes to be tracked, defines the three mandatory data points in each four-hour snapshot, and ties the escalation mechanism to an automated dashboard alert — preventing the four-hour cadence from becoming a manual ad-hoc report.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Proactively communicate May 25-29 delivery-delay expectations to all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 06:00 CET on 25 May 2026, the Customer Experience Operations Manager must issue proactive delay communications to all Italian and cross-border customers with active purchase orders containing Italian road delivery legs scheduled between 25 May and 29 May, referencing the UNATRAS 120-hour national road haulage stoppage as the cause and providing: a revised estimated time of arrival for each affected order, the name and direct contact of the assigned escalation manager, and the company's planned mitigation (intermodal or cross-border re-routing where applicable). Communications must be dispatched through the customer order management portal and/or direct email, with a dispatch log — recording customer name, order reference, revised ETA, and timestamp — filed in the CRM system before 06:00. Evidence of completion is the CRM dispatch log with all records timestamped at or before 06:00 on 25 May.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires customer delay notification with revised ETAs before 06:00 on 25 May; this adaptation specifies that each communication must name the UNATRAS action as cause, include the mitigation mode where one has been arranged, and be evidenced by a per-order CRM log entry — ensuring notifications are substantive and auditable rather than a single broadcast message.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate SLA-breach tracking for all customer contracts covering
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: From 00:01 on 25 May 2026, the Contract Performance Manager must activate SLA-breach tracking for every customer contract containing Italian road delivery leg commitments, flagging each shipment whose confirmed or projected delivery date during 25–29 May exceeds the contracted SLA threshold; a breach log must be maintained in the contract management system, updated at each of the Control Tower's four-hour intervals, recording customer name, contract reference, SLA threshold, actual or projected delay, and breach status. By 30 May 2026 no later than 17:00 CET, the Contract Performance Manager must present to the Senior Logistics Director a consolidated post-event breach report and a schedule of remediation offers — including applicable service credits, priority re-delivery slots, or written apology letters — for all customers where the SLA threshold was crossed during the stoppage window. Evidence of completion is the live breach log updated throughout 25–29 May and the post-event report filed on 30 May.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control activates SLA-breach tracking and prepares remediation offers; this adaptation ties breach log updates to the existing four-hour Control Tower cycle, mandates a 30 May deadline for the post-event remediation schedule, and specifies the minimum data fields per log entry — integrating SLA monitoring into the incident operational rhythm rather than treating it as a separate parallel process.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Confirm legal status of employed drivers who are
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 23:00 CET on 24 May 2026, the Head of HR — Italy must obtain a written legal opinion from Italian employment counsel confirming the status of all employed drivers not affiliated with UNATRAS member associations (Cna Fita, Confartigianato Trasporti, Fai, Fiap, Sna/Casartigiani, Unitai, Agci, Legacoop Produzione e Servizi, Confcooperative Lavoro e Servizi) under Law 146/1990 essential-service provisions, specifying whether and under what conditions those drivers may be required or are permitted to continue operations during the 25–29 May stoppage. Based on that opinion, the Head of HR — Italy must issue written guidance to the relevant fleet and operations managers by 00:01 on 25 May covering: each driver's legal obligation or right to work, any essential-service routes mandated by the Commissione di Garanzia, and the disciplinary or liability consequences of non-compliance in either direction. Evidence of completion is the retained legal opinion and the timestamped written guidance distributed before 00:01 on 25 May.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires confirmation of employed non-member drivers' legal status under Law 146/1990; this adaptation names all nine UNATRAS member associations so that the assessment scope is unambiguous, sets a 23:00 legal-opinion deadline separate from the 00:01 guidance-issuance deadline, and requires the guidance to address consequences of non-compliance — making the legal assessment actionable for operational managers.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Brief logistics workforce and management on the UNATRAS
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within the first 24 hours of stoppage commencement (i.e. by 00:01 on 26 May 2026), the Italian Logistics HR Business Partner must deliver a structured briefing — by in-person session, video call, or written brief with confirmation of receipt — to all logistics workforce members and their line managers in Italy, covering: the confirmed scope of the UNATRAS 120-hour stoppage (25 May 00:01 to 29 May 24:00), the identity of the nine promoting associations under the UNATRAS umbrella, employees' rights and obligations under Italian Law 146/1990, the company's contingency operating model for the period (including intermodal and cross-border re-routing arrangements), and the escalation contact for workforce queries. Attendance or receipt must be recorded and filed in the HRIS by 00:01 on 26 May. Evidence of completion is the HRIS attendance/receipt log and the dated briefing materials retained in the HR document management system.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires workforce briefing within 24 hours of the stoppage; this adaptation specifies the five mandatory content areas — including naming the nine UNATRAS associations and the Law 146/1990 obligations — sets 00:01 on 26 May as the hard deadline, and requires HRIS documentation of individual receipt, preventing the briefing from being satisfied by a single unanswered all-staff email.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Monitor Commissione di Garanzia orders and Italian Ministry
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: For each calendar day from 25 May through 29 May 2026, the Regulatory Intelligence Analyst must monitor and review by 09:00 CET: (a) published orders or communications from the Commissione di Garanzia dell'attuazione della legge sullo sciopero nei servizi pubblici essenziali regarding any mandated essential-service resumptions applicable to road freight, and (b) official communications from the Italian Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport (MIT) that alter the scope, duration, or exemptions of the UNATRAS stoppage. Any order or communication that modifies the stoppage scope must be summarised in a written flash-alert distributed to the Senior Logistics Director, Head of HR — Italy, and Business Continuity Lead within two hours of publication, identifying the specific obligation created and the operational adjustment required. Evidence of completion is the daily monitoring log filed in the incident management system by 09:00 each day and any flash-alerts issued, each with timestamps.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires daily monitoring of Commissione di Garanzia and MIT communications; this adaptation sets a 09:00 daily review deadline, names both the Commissione di Garanzia and MIT as the specific monitoring targets, and mandates a two-hour flash-alert to named senior stakeholders when any scope-altering order is identified — ensuring regulatory developments are operationalised within the incident response cycle rather than logged passively.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess exposure of cross-border supply flows transiting Italian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 20:00 CET on 24 May 2026, the Cross-Border Supply Chain Manager must complete a written exposure assessment for all supply flows transiting Italian Alpine road corridors — specifically Brenner Pass (A22/E45), Mont Blanc Tunnel (A5), and Fréjus Tunnel (A32) — that are scheduled for the 25–29 May window, identifying for each corridor: current booking volumes, available alternative routing options (including Swiss Gotthard road/rail and Austrian rail intermodal), estimated transit-time and cost delta, and any cross-border carrier capacity constraints. The assessment must classify each active flow as (a) successfully pre-re-routed, (b) requiring re-routing action before 00:01 on 25 May, or (c) assessed as unaffected by the Italian road halt. The completed assessment must be submitted to the Senior Logistics Director by 20:00 on 24 May, and any flow classified as requiring action must have a confirmed re-routing booking in the TMS before 00:01 on 25 May. Evidence of completion is the filed written assessment and the TMS booking records.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control directs assessment of Alpine corridor exposure in general terms; this adaptation names all three specific road tunnels and passes, requires classification of each flow into one of three action categories, and imposes a 20:00 assessment deadline followed by a 00:01 booking-confirmation deadline — converting a passive exposure review into a time-bounded decision and action sequence.

incidents.headline: Adobe CEO Shantanu Narayen plans exit amid AI restructuring
incidents.summary: Adobe CEO Shantanu Narayen — who has led the company since 2007 and overseen its transformation from boxed software to cloud SaaS — announced plans to exit as Adobe restructures its business around artificial intelligence, according to reports captured in executive-tracking sources updated in the May 20–25, 2026 window. Narayen's planned departure comes after Adobe's $20 billion bid to acquire Figma was abandoned in December 2023 following EU and UK regulatory blocks, and as the company faces intensifying competitive disruption from generative AI tools threatening its flagship Creative Cloud products. The leadership change at one of the world's most recognisable creative-software franchises signals a strategic inflection point for the company's AI positioning.

secondary_mappings.why: Narayen's exit is explicitly linked to the need to restructure Adobe around AI, reflecting existential disruption of its core creative-software business model by generative AI competitors.

Adobe faces direct competitive threat to Creative Cloud from generative AI tools (Midjourney, OpenAI, Stability AI, Canva) that undermine the pricing power and switching costs of its flagship product portfolio.
Departure of a 17-year incumbent CEO during a period of competitive disruption and failed M&A (Figma) will heighten investor uncertainty about strategic direction and succession quality.

incidents.severity_rationale: CEO departure at a major S&P 500 software company undergoing AI-driven business model disruption, compounded by prior failed $20B M&A, constitutes a meaningful strategic governance event, though Adobe remains profitable and the transition appears planned.

sources.title: Latest CEO Appointments and Changes in Leading Companies 2026

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure Adobe's Board-led CEO succession process produces a named, credible successor with a defined AI-strategy mandate before competitive disruption from Figma and Canva materially erodes Creative Cloud market share.
Preserve strategic continuity of Adobe's AI product roadmap — specifically Firefly commercialisation and the OpenAI ChatGPT integration announced in Q1 FY2026 — through the CEO transition period.
Accelerate Adobe's generative AI innovation posture so that annualised Firefly AI-first product revenue growth (currently tripling YoY) is sustained under any incoming CEO without a reset period.
Defend Adobe's Creative Cloud market position against Canva's free Affinity relaunch and Figma's enterprise expansion by maintaining go-to-market velocity during the succession window.
Manage the reputational and investor-confidence impact of a CEO departure coinciding with a 23-25% YTD share decline, ensuring stakeholder communications do not amplify 'SaaS-mageddon' sentiment.

graded_claims.claim: Shantanu Narayen announced he will step down as Adobe CEO after a successor is appointed.
Narayen will remain as Chair of the Board following CEO transition.
Narayen has served as CEO of Adobe for eighteen years, since 2007.
Frank Calderoni, Lead Independent Director, chairs the special succession committee considering internal and external candidates.
Adobe shares fell approximately 7% in extended trading following the announcement.
Adobe Q1 FY2026 revenue was approximately $6.4 billion, up 12.1% year-over-year, beating analyst estimates.
Adobe shares are down approximately 23-25% year-to-date as of the announcement date.
Narayen's departure was caused at least in part by investor impatience with Adobe's AI transition.
Annualized revenue from Adobe's AI-first products more than tripled year-over-year in Q1 FY2026.
A broad 'SaaS-mageddon' sell-off in early February 2026 reflected fears that agentic AI could undermine per-seat software pricing.
Adobe's $20 billion bid to acquire Figma was abandoned in December 2023 following EU and UK regulatory blocks.
Narayen received $51 million in total compensation for fiscal year 2025 and owns $118 million in Adobe shares.
Adobe announced availability of Acrobat, Express and Photoshop apps for OpenAI's ChatGPT assistant during Q1 FY2026.
Canva is widely rumored to pursue an IPO in 2026 and relaunched its Affinity suite as a free all-in-one app.
