adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update sanctions screening lists to incorporate the Russian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 23, 2026 (one business day before the transaction-ban effective date), the Head of Sanctions Compliance must certify that screening systems have been updated to enforce a categorical block on all Russian-established CASPs under Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506, rather than relying solely on named-entity lists — ensuring that any counterparty whose place of establishment is Russia triggers an automatic hold regardless of whether it appears on a discrete designation list. Evidence of completion is a signed configuration change record from the transaction screening platform confirming the jurisdiction-of-establishment rule is live, accompanied by a test-case log demonstrating that a synthetic Russian-established CASP counterparty is correctly blocked.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The 20th package's deliberate shift to a categorical jurisdictional ban — not an entity-list model — means screening systems calibrated to named-platform alerts will structurally fail to catch newly established or renamed Russian CASPs, the precise evasion vector the regulation was designed to close.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate geo-blocking and counterparty exclusion controls for all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 23, 2026, the Chief Compliance Officer must activate geo-blocking and counterparty exclusion rules across all trading, settlement, and API-onboarding systems that reject — at the point of initiation — any transaction with a CASP whose registered or operational jurisdiction is Russia or Belarus, without awaiting publication of an updated named-entity designation list; the control must be parameterised to treat Russian or Belarusian jurisdiction of establishment as a disqualifying attribute equivalent to OFAC SDN status. Completion evidence is a system configuration attestation, a parallel-run report showing zero false negatives against a test set of known Russian-established CASPs (including Garantex wallet cluster proxies), and written sign-off from the Head of Financial Crime.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The EU 20th package's ecosystem-level logic — banning an entire jurisdictional category rather than named platforms — renders entity-by-entity blocking insufficient and requires a jurisdiction-attribute-based exclusion layer that prior sanction packages did not necessitate.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Escalate to Board or relevant risk committee any
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Effective May 24, 2026, any transaction, settlement instruction, or collateral position involving A7A5, RUBx, or the digital ruble — each listed in EU Annex LIII under the 20th package — must be automatically escalated to the Board Risk Committee within four business hours of detection, with a freeze applied to the relevant position pending committee disposition; the escalation pack must include the on-chain wallet address or counterparty identifier, notional exposure in EUR, and a legal opinion paragraph confirming prohibited-instrument status under Annex LIII. The Chief Risk Officer is accountable for maintaining the escalation workflow, and evidence of operationalisation is a tested escalation run completed before May 24 using synthetic A7A5-flagged transaction data.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The explicit EU Annex LIII listing of A7A5, RUBx, and the digital ruble — including a preemptive ban on the digital ruble ahead of Russia's planned September 2026 CBDC rollout — creates named-instrument prohibited-exposure triggers that require board-level visibility rather than routine compliance-team disposition.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a jurisdiction-level risk reassessment of Central Asian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within ten business days of May 24, 2026, the Head of Third-Party Risk must complete a jurisdiction-level risk reassessment of all counterparty corridors involving Kyrgyzstan (anchored by the TengriCoin/Meer.kg designation), Azerbaijan, and Laos, producing a tiered exposure map that identifies any direct or correspondent relationship with entities in those jurisdictions and quantifies the volume of flows that transited through those corridors in the 12 months prior to May 24; any counterparty rated elevated or critical in the reassessment must be escalated to the Sanctions Compliance Committee with a recommended action (suspend, enhanced due diligence, or terminate) within five additional business days. Evidence of completion is the signed reassessment report, the tiered exposure map, and minutes of the Sanctions Compliance Committee meeting recording disposition decisions.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The EU's first-ever activation of its anti-circumvention mechanism against Kyrgyzstan — specifically designating TengriCoin/Meer.kg as a named third-country platform — signals that the three jurisdictions are now treated as active circumvention corridors, requiring a jurisdiction-level rather than entity-level reassessment framework.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Quantify maximum penalty exposure under Council Regulation (EU)
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Before the next scheduled Board meeting following May 24, 2026 (and no later than June 30, 2026), the Chief Financial Officer, supported by Legal, must present to the Board a quantified penalty exposure scenario under Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506 calculated as the higher of 5% of the firm's most recent audited global annual turnover or €40 million, stress-tested against three scenarios: (a) a single inadvertent post-May 24 transaction with a Russian-established CASP, (b) continued settlement of pre-existing netting arrangements with indirect Russian CASP exposure, and (c) holding of A7A5 or RUBx instruments past the Annex LIII effective date; the Board must formally record its risk appetite disposition against each scenario in the updated risk appetite statement. Evidence of completion is the Board-approved risk appetite statement amendment and the supporting penalty quantification model, both retained in the Board pack for the relevant meeting.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The 20th package introduces a specific and novel dual-limb penalty structure — 5% of global turnover or €40 million — that has not previously appeared in EU crypto-sanctions enforcement and must be explicitly modelled rather than estimated by analogy to prior packages.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Model the revenue impact of de-platforming Russian-established CASP
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 21, 2026 (three business days before the transaction-ban effective date), the Head of Financial Planning must deliver to the CFO and CCO a revenue-impact model disaggregating, by business line, all income attributable to Russian-established CASP counterparties that must be de-platformed under the May 24 ban, including fee revenue, spread income, custody fees, and any liquidity-provision arrangements; the model must identify which revenue streams can be wound down by May 24 and which require a structured exit that must nonetheless be completed by that date, given the absence of any grandfathering provision in Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506. Evidence of completion is the signed model document with business-line breakdowns, a wind-down timeline per counterparty, and confirmation from the CCO that the timeline is legally compliant.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Unlike prior EU sanction packages that named specific platforms and allowed market participants to plan exits in advance, the categorical Russian CASP ban under the 20th package potentially sweeps in counterparties not individually tracked as sanctions risks, requiring a revenue attribution exercise before — not after — the wind-down deadline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement a regulatory-change trigger linked to the EU
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 16, 2026, the Head of Regulatory Affairs must configure the firm's regulatory-change management system to monitor the EU Official Journal for any amendments to Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506 and auto-route detected publications to the Legal, Compliance, and Financial Crime functions within 24 hours of posting, with a mandatory acknowledgement receipt required from each function head before the alert can be closed; the trigger must be tested using a sandboxed simulation of an Official Journal amendment publication before going live. Evidence of completion is the system configuration record, the test-run output log showing 24-hour routing and receipt confirmation, and a process note signed by the Head of Regulatory Affairs.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506 introduced a structural innovation — the categorical CASP ban and the anti-circumvention mechanism — that the EU may amend rapidly as evasion patterns evolve, making a reactive monitoring posture (checking periodically or waiting for industry alerts) insufficient compared to an automated Official Journal trigger.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Map all MiCA authorisation conditions against the EU
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 30, 2026, the Head of Regulatory Compliance must produce a gap analysis mapping each MiCA authorisation condition against the enhanced CASP compliance obligations implied by the EU 20th package's bank-grade parity requirement, identifying any condition where the firm's current MiCA-compliant posture falls short of the additional obligations introduced by Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506 — specifically covering transaction monitoring depth, sanctions screening architecture, and counterparty due diligence standards — and present remediation actions with owners and deadlines to the Risk Committee before the AMLR application date of July 10, 2026. Evidence of completion is the signed gap analysis, a remediation tracker with assigned owners, and Risk Committee minutes recording acceptance of the remediation plan.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The 20th package's bank-grade parity requirement for CASPs creates compliance obligations that exceed baseline MiCA authorisation conditions, meaning a firm that achieved MiCA authorisation in good standing may nonetheless be non-compliant with the new sanctions framework without a specific bridging analysis.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Prepare a compliance attestation for the relevant NCA
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 28, 2026 (four business days after the May 24 effective date), the Chief Compliance Officer must submit to the relevant National Competent Authority a written compliance attestation confirming: (a) transaction monitoring rules have been updated to flag and block interactions with Russian-established CASPs as a category under Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506, (b) the counterparty database has been refreshed to apply the jurisdictional-establishment attribute screen, (c) A7A5, RUBx, and digital ruble instruments are flagged as prohibited in the firm's asset catalogue per EU Annex LIII, and (d) TengriCoin/Meer.kg is blocked as a specifically designated third-country platform; the attestation must reference the configuration change records and test-run logs as supporting annexes. Evidence of completion is the submitted attestation with NCA delivery confirmation and the supporting annexes filed in the compliance record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The 20th package's introduction of the categorical CASP ban — rather than the entity-list model used in prior packages — means regulators require affirmative attestation that the firm's control architecture has been re-engineered for jurisdictional screening, not merely that individual named platforms have been added to block lists.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Configure transaction monitoring rulesets in the blockchain analytics
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 23, 2026, the Head of Financial Crime Technology must deploy, in the firm's blockchain analytics platform, dedicated detection rulesets that flag as prohibited any on-chain transaction touching wallet addresses or smart contract addresses attributable to A7A5, RUBx, or digital ruble infrastructure — as catalogued in EU Annex LIII — generating a real-time alert routed to the Sanctions Operations desk with an automatic hold on the associated transaction pending review; the rulesets must cover both direct interactions and second-hop transactions where the firm's wallet transacts with a counterparty that has transacted with an Annex LIII cluster within the prior 30 days. Evidence of completion is a configuration deployment record, a test-run report against a known A7A5 wallet address demonstrating correct alert and hold generation, and sign-off from the Head of Financial Crime Technology.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The A7A5 stablecoin ecosystem's role as a settlement rail — reported to have processed $119.7 billion and to remain partially operational even after volume declines — means generic sanctions screening is insufficient; ruleset-level on-chain detection with second-hop coverage is required to catch layered exposure through intermediary wallets.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Enable the 'Russian CASP Transaction Ban' risk category
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 22, 2026, the Head of Blockchain Intelligence must enable the 'Russian CASP Transaction Ban' risk category in TRM Labs (or the firm's equivalent blockchain analytics provider if TRM Labs is not deployed) and run a validation exercise confirming that all known Garantex-successor and Grinex-associated wallet clusters are captured within that category's alert scope — producing a coverage report that maps each identified cluster to its attribution evidence and confirms no material wallet cluster identified in EU Council rationale documentation falls outside the ruleset; any gaps identified in the validation must be escalated to the CCO and remediated before May 24. Evidence of completion is the TRM Labs (or equivalent) category activation log, the validation coverage report, the gap remediation record if applicable, and CCO sign-off.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The Garantex-to-Grinex successor migration pattern — which the EU 20th package explicitly cited in its Council rationale — demonstrates that sanctioned Russian CASP infrastructure actively relaunches under new entity names, requiring validation against successor-cluster attribution rather than reliance on the provider's default category coverage.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Perform enhanced due diligence on all third-party CASPs
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 15 business days of May 24, 2026, the Third-Party Risk team must complete enhanced due diligence reviews on all CASPs and OTC desks in the firm's counterparty registry whose registered address, primary operating jurisdiction, or predominant transaction flow is associated with Kyrgyzstan, Laos, or Azerbaijan — the three jurisdictions hosting EU-designated third-country banks and platforms under the 20th package — specifically assessing whether each counterparty maintains correspondent relationships with, or settlement exposure to, any Russian-established CASP or to TengriCoin/Meer.kg; counterparties where indirect Russian CASP nexus cannot be ruled out after enhanced due diligence must be suspended pending a Sanctions Compliance Committee decision. Evidence of completion is a completed EDD report for each in-scope counterparty, a nexus determination (confirmed, ruled out, or inconclusive), and a Sanctions Compliance Committee disposition record for any suspended counterparty.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The EU's designation of TengriCoin/Meer.kg and third-country banks in Kyrgyzstan, Laos, and Azerbaijan — the first use of the anti-circumvention mechanism — establishes those three jurisdictions as confirmed circumvention corridors, meaning existing counterparty due diligence that did not screen for Russian CASP nexus is structurally insufficient and must be re-run with that specific lens.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Audit netting and settlement arrangements with Central Asian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 21, 2026, the Head of Correspondent Banking and Settlement Risk must complete an audit of all netting and settlement arrangements with Central Asian and Gulf correspondent CASPs, specifically testing whether any netting agreement results in a net payment to or from a Russian payment agent or a Russian-established CASP — the circumvention vector explicitly prohibited under Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506 — and must suspend any arrangement where such a flow cannot be affirmatively excluded; a written legal opinion confirming that each remaining active netting arrangement does not constitute a prohibited transaction under the regulation must be obtained and filed before May 24. Evidence of completion is the audit report listing all reviewed arrangements, the suspension log for any paused arrangements, and filed legal opinions for all continuing arrangements.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The EU 20th package's explicit prohibition on netting transactions used for circumvention — a structural mechanism through which sanctioned Russian payment agents have moved value through Central Asian and Gulf correspondent chains — requires a targeted audit of netting architecture, which standard counterparty KYC reviews do not examine at the transaction-flow level.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Add a contractual sanctions-compliance clause to all CASP
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 24, 2026, the Head of Legal must issue amended contractual notices to all CASP liquidity-provider counterparties in the firm's active agreement registry, inserting a sanctions-compliance clause that requires the counterparty to notify the firm within 24 hours if: (a) the counterparty is or becomes Russian-established within the meaning of Council Regulation (EU) 2026/506, (b) the counterparty holds, settles, or provides liquidity in A7A5, RUBx, or digital ruble instruments listed in EU Annex LIII, or (c) the counterparty has a correspondent or netting relationship with TengriCoin/Meer.kg or any entity on the 20th package designation list; failure to notify must be designated a material breach triggering immediate suspension rights. Evidence of completion is a log of all counterparties notified, confirmation of delivery, and a signed clause template reviewed by external sanctions counsel.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The categorical nature of the Russian CASP ban — applying to any Russian-established entity, not a finite named list — means counterparties themselves may not recognise their own non-compliance, making contractual self-reporting obligations with explicit instrument-level and designation-level triggers a necessary supplement to the firm's own screening.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement successor-platform detection monitoring — triggered by the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 23, 2026, the Head of Financial Crime Intelligence must operationalise a successor-platform detection monitoring process — parameterised specifically on the Garantex-to-Grinex migration pattern documented in the EU Council rationale for the 20th package — that continuously monitors for newly formed or rebranded entities exhibiting: shared wallet cluster infrastructure with Garantex or Grinex, similar on-chain transaction patterns (including A7A5 settlement rails), or registration in Russian-adjacent jurisdictions (Kyrgyzstan, Belarus, Laos, and Azerbaijan) without a prior EU designation; any entity matching two or more of these indicators must be treated as a presumptive Russian-established CASP and escalated to the Sanctions Operations desk for blocking pending confirmation; if the reported Grinex cyberattack-related operational halt is independently confirmed, the monitoring scope must be expanded to include wallet clusters associated with the reported residual A7 issuer network. Evidence of completion is the documented detection logic, an initial scan report against known Garantex and Grinex wallet clusters, and a live-monitoring dashboard accessible to the Sanctions Operations desk from May 24.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The confirmed Garantex-to-Grinex succession demonstrates that Russian CASP infrastructure migrates to new legal entities faster than EU designation cycles, making post-designation blocking insufficient and requiring a behavioural and infrastructure pattern-matching detection layer that activates before formal designation.

incidents.headline: Strait of Hormuz Remains Effectively Closed — UK Deploys Military Assets
incidents.summary: As of May 24–25, 2026, the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial shipping following Iran's blockade initiated on February 28, 2026, in retaliation for the US-Israeli Operation Epic Fury strikes; vessel traffic stands at approximately 5% of the pre-conflict monthly average of ~3,000 transits. A conditional US-Iran ceasefire is in place but remains fragile, with Iran seizing sanctioned tankers, demanding toll payments of up to $2 million per vessel, and the IRGC continuing to board and attack merchant ships. The UK has confirmed deployment of drones, fighter aircraft, and a Royal Navy warship to join an international defensive escort mission to restore freedom of navigation, with the House of Commons Library publishing an updated briefing within the 24-hour window.
incidents.entity: Global commercial shipping (primary victim); Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) as threat actor disrupting ~20% of world seaborne oil and LNG trade

secondary_mappings.why: OFAC issued a formal alert warning shippers that paying Iranian transit tolls constitutes a sanctions violation; the US blockade of Iranian ports and ongoing SDN designations of tankers and Iranian exchange houses are active and continuing into the window.
Closure of the strait disrupts 20% of global seaborne crude oil, 20% of LNG, and 30% of internationally traded fertilisers, causing sustained global supply chain shock.
Iran has seized vessels including the Ocean Koi tanker and attacked multiple merchant ships including the CMA CGM San Antonio, constituting physical asset seizure and attacks on commercial shipping.
Prolonged Hormuz closure has driven sustained energy price inflation, tightening liquidity across energy-importing economies and materially affecting corporate treasury hedging positions.
Shipping firms face reputational and legal risk from either paying Iranian tolls (sanctions exposure) or refusing transit (stranded cargo/crew and contractual default).

incidents.severity_rationale: Ongoing closure of a chokepoint carrying 20% of global seaborne oil and LNG, active military engagement, vessel seizures and attacks, and a fragile ceasefire constitute the highest-severity GEO event in the sweep; confirmed still active and updating within the 24-hour window.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure the organisation can maintain operational continuity and protect personnel and assets when a primary maritime transit corridor (Strait of Hormuz) is rendered commercially impassable by state-actor interdiction.

Ensure the organisation identifies, monitors, and responds to deteriorating geopolitical conditions — including a conditional US-Iran ceasefire of acknowledged fragility — that materially affect access to critical trade routes.

Ensure supply-chain and operational continuity plans are activated and validated when a primary routing dependency (Hormuz, carrying 20% of world seaborne oil and LNG) is disrupted for greater than 30 days.

Ensure physical security of company vessels, cargo, and crew operating in or near the Strait of Hormuz, where the IRGC is conducting armed boardings, missile strikes, and mine deployment against commercial shipping.

Ensure financial exposures arising from Hormuz closure — including war-risk insurance cancellation from March 5, toll-payment sanctions exposure, and force majeure revenue loss — are quantified and disclosed.
Ensure treasury and working-capital resilience is maintained against sustained extraordinary costs (e.g. Hapag-Lloyd's disclosed $60M/week) arising directly from the Hormuz operational disruption.

Ensure timely, accurate stakeholder communication regarding the company's exposure to the Hormuz closure, including force majeure notifications, vessel status, and crew safety, to protect counterparty confidence.

graded_claims.claim: The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to commercial shipping since Iran's blockade initiated on February 28, 2026, following US-Israeli Operation Epic Fury strikes.
Vessel traffic through the strait stands at approximately 5% of the pre-conflict monthly average of approximately 3,000 transits.
The Strait of Hormuz carries approximately 20% of the world's seaborne oil and LNG trade.
Iran is demanding toll payments of up to $2 million per vessel for passage through its designated channel, with payments assessed by the IRGC in Chinese yuan.
The IRGC is continuing to board and attack merchant ships, including firing on the Indian-flagged Sanmar Herald on April 18, 2026, despite that vessel having prior IRGC clearance.
A conditional US-Iran ceasefire is in place but remains fragile.

The UK has confirmed deployment of HMS Dragon (Type 45 destroyer), drones, and fighter aircraft to join an international defensive escort mission; the deployment is backed by £115 million in new funding.
Hapag-Lloyd AG publicly stated the Hormuz closure is costing it $60 million per week, with four ships stranded inside the Persian Gulf.
Protection and indemnity (war-risk) insurance was cancelled from March 5, 2026; premiums jumped from under 1% to 3-10% of vessel value.
QatarEnergy declared force majeure on all LNG shipments on March 4, 2026, after Iranian attacks on its Ras Laffan facilities.
More than 1,550 commercial vessels with approximately 22,500 mariners remain stranded inside the Persian Gulf as of late May 2026.
Paying IRGC toll fees risks violating US and EU sanctions, as both have designated the IRGC a terrorist organisation.
The US has stated it will take six months to clear mines believed to have been laid by Iran in the strait.
CMA CGM container ship CMA CGM Everglade was damaged in a rocket attack off the coast of Oman.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate the geopolitical-risk escalation protocol for the Strait of Hormuz
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Risk Officer must formally declare the IRGC blockade of the Strait of Hormuz — in effect since February 28, 2026, and reducing transits to approximately 5% of the pre-conflict monthly average of 3,000 — a Tier-1 strategic disruption in the company's enterprise risk register by close of business May 27, 2026. Board-level oversight must be activated with a standing agenda item at each board meeting until Hormuz transits recover above 50% of the pre-conflict baseline, with a written situation report covering IRGC interdiction activity, ceasefire status, and the UK escort mission scope delivered to the board no later than each Monday at 09:00 UTC. Evidence of completion: signed board resolution, updated risk register entry referencing the February 28 blockade trigger, and distribution records for the first weekly report.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires Tier-1 classification and board oversight; this adaptation pins the escalation trigger to the confirmed February 28 blockade date, specifies the 5%-of-3,000-transits severity threshold, and mandates weekly Monday reporting cadence aligned to the live UK escort mission deployment rather than leaving cadence undefined.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain a live intelligence feed from Lloyd's List
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Maritime Intelligence must establish a continuous monitoring feed drawing from Lloyd's List Intelligence vessel-tracking data and UKMTO Maritime Security Communications with Industry (MSCI) advisories specifically covering the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf approach lanes, with automated ingestion into the company's routing decision log. Any change in the conditional US-Iran ceasefire status — including partial collapse, extension, or renegotiation — must trigger a revised routing guidance bulletin issued to all fleet operators and cargo desks within 24 hours, explicitly addressing whether the UK escort corridor under HMS Dragon's deployment remains operationally available for the company's stranded or inbound vessels. Evidence of completion: feed integration confirmation with timestamped data receipt logs, and a routing bulletin version-control register showing issuance within 24 hours of each ceasefire status event.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a live intelligence feed and 24-hour update cycle; this adaptation specifies the exact UKMTO and Lloyd's List Intelligence sources relevant to the Hormuz corridor, links the update obligation directly to ceasefire status changes affecting the fragile US-Iran agreement, and ties routing guidance to the operational status of HMS Dragon's escort mission rather than a generic geopolitical trigger.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Scenario-plan three geopolitical exit paths — ceasefire collapse
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Strategy Officer must commission and deliver to the Executive Committee by June 6, 2026 a written scenario-planning document covering exactly three exit paths from the current Hormuz closure: (1) ceasefire collapse, defined as the resumption of IRGC offensive operations at pre-ceasefire intensity or Iran's withdrawal from the conditional agreement, triggering full rerouting via Cape of Good Hope and suspension of all Gulf entry orders; (2) durable ceasefire with a functioning UK/coalition escort corridor, defined as HMS Dragon and allied assets achieving two or more consecutive weeks of uncontested merchant transits, triggering slot-allocation requests and hardened-vessel preparation; and (3) a negotiated Iranian toll regime, defined as any formalised payment framework replacing the current ad-hoc IRGC demand of up to $2 million per vessel in Chinese yuan, triggering immediate legal counsel review against OFAC SDN and EU/UK IRGC designation rules before any company vessel enters the designated channel. Each scenario must include documented financial triggers — minimum liquidity buffer, maximum acceptable insurance premium as a percentage of vessel value, and cargo-contract renegotiation deadlines — anchored against the confirmed $60 million-per-week cost rate disclosed by Hapag-Lloyd AG. Evidence of completion: dated scenario document, ExCo sign-off minutes, and named trigger owners for each scenario.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires three-path scenario planning with financial triggers; this adaptation locks the three scenarios to the specific confirmed conditions of May 24, 2026, names the HMS Dragon corridor as the durable-ceasefire operationalisation, ties the toll-regime scenario to the confirmed IRGC yuan-denominated $2 million demand and its sanctions exposure, and anchors financial thresholds to the Hapag-Lloyd-confirmed $60 million weekly cost benchmark.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Engage the UK-led multinational defensive escort mission planning
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Fleet Operations must, by May 28, 2026, formally register each company vessel currently stranded among the confirmed 1,550+ commercial ships inside the Persian Gulf with the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) liaison coordinating the HMS Dragon-led international escort mission, submitting vessel name, IMO number, flag state, current position, crew count, and cargo classification for convoy slot prioritisation. The Fleet Operations team must maintain direct communication with the escort mission's designated maritime coordination authority at intervals not exceeding 48 hours and must document each vessel's queue position, estimated escort window, and any IRGC contact or boarding notification received since February 28, 2026. Evidence of completion: submitted UKMTO registration confirmations for each stranded vessel, a maintained vessel status log with 48-hour update timestamps, and records of any IRGC contact reported to the coordination authority.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires engagement with the escort mission planning process; this adaptation specifies the UKMTO registration mechanism, requires submission of operationally essential vessel data matched to the confirmed 1,550+ stranded-vessel context, and imposes a 48-hour communication discipline reflecting the dynamic IRGC boarding environment documented since February 28.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Obtain explicit legal counsel opinion on whether paying
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Before any company vessel is directed to enter the IRGC-designated toll channel or any payment is authorised in response to IRGC demands, the General Counsel must obtain a written legal opinion — from external sanctions counsel with OFAC and EU regulatory practice — specifically addressing whether remitting any portion of the IRGC's published toll of up to $2 million per vessel, denominated in Chinese yuan, constitutes a prohibited transaction under US OFAC's SDN designation of the IRGC, the EU's IRGC terrorist designation, and the UK's equivalent proscription, including analysis of any pennalty exposure for intermediary financial institutions processing the yuan-denominated transfer. This opinion must be obtained and approved by the General Counsel and CFO in writing before any transit instruction is issued, and must be refreshed within 72 hours if ceasefire conditions or OFAC guidance change materially. Evidence of completion: dated external legal opinion on file, General Counsel and CFO co-signature on the transit authorisation gate, and a register of opinion refresh dates tied to ceasefire or regulatory developments.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires legal counsel review of sanctions exposure; this adaptation specifies the exact payment structure under review — the confirmed IRGC yuan-denominated $2 million toll — and requires the opinion to address all three relevant designating jurisdictions (US, EU, UK) as well as the intermediary bank exposure created by the yuan-denomination, which is operationally critical and not captured in a generic sanctions-check directive.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate force majeure clauses in all voyage charter
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Commercial and Legal must, by May 29, 2026, issue formal force majeure notices under all voyage charter parties and cargo contracts covering Hormuz-routed shipments, citing two confirmed triggering events: the IRGC blockade effective February 28, 2026, and the confirmed withdrawal of war-risk (Protection and Indemnity) insurance coverage effective March 5, 2026, which together render performance legally and practically impossible. Notices must be sent to all counterparties with contractual cure windows expiring after February 28 and must reference QatarEnergy's confirmed force majeure declaration on LNG shipments dated March 4, 2026 as a precedent in the same trade corridor where relevant. The Head of Commercial must maintain a contract-by-contract tracker showing notice date, counterparty acknowledgement, any disputed claims, and the financial exposure of each contract in abeyance. Evidence of completion: dated notice copies for each affected contract, counterparty acknowledgement log, and a commercial exposure register reviewed by the CFO by June 3, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires force majeure activation; this adaptation names both confirmed triggering events with their precise dates, incorporates the QatarEnergy precedent as a corridor-specific reference point for counterparty negotiations, and requires a contract-by-contract tracker that captures financial exposure — making the control executable against a specific portfolio rather than a generic clause-activation directive.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Reroute all non-energy cargo with viable alternatives to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Fleet Operations must, within 72 hours of each non-energy cargo vessel's scheduled or actual Persian Gulf entry date, issue a documented rerouting decision to cargo clients for all voyages where Cape of Good Hope diversion or Saudi East-West Petroline (Petroline) landbridge connection is operationally viable, explicitly stating the estimated additional transit time, revised ETA, and incremental freight cost, referencing the confirmed closure of the Strait of Hormuz since February 28, 2026 as the operative cause. For vessels already inside the Gulf where rerouting is no longer available, the decision record must instead document the escort-convoy application status and current estimated delay based on the approximately 5% transit rate through the strait. All decisions must be communicated to cargo clients in writing, with a named operations contact for queries, and logged in the company's voyage management system with a compliance timestamp. Evidence of completion: rerouting decision log entries with 72-hour timestamps, client communication records, and voyage management system audit trail.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires rerouting decisions within 72 hours; this adaptation distinguishes between pre-Gulf-entry vessels (where diversion is actionable) and already-stranded vessels (where the decision record must instead capture escort queue status), ties the client communication to the confirmed 5% transit throughput that explains delay severity, and specifies Petroline as a named alternative rather than leaving the routing option abstract.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a vessel welfare and crew-relief plan for the 22
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Crew Welfare and the Head of Fleet Operations must jointly, by May 30, 2026, produce and activate a vessel welfare and crew-relief plan covering all company seafarers among the confirmed approximately 22,500 mariners stranded inside the Persian Gulf, specifying: (a) a maximum crew rotation interval of 90 days from the February 28, 2026 blockade commencement date, with emergency air-evacuation arrangements for any seafarer exceeding that interval via flag-state naval attaché coordination or UAE/Oman port authority medical transfer; (b) a vessel-by-vessel supply and provisions schedule ensuring food, water, and medical consumables for a minimum 60-day forward buffer given the reported US estimate of six months to clear believed Iranian mines; and (c) a formal psychological support protocol delivered by qualified maritime welfare organisations (e.g. Mission to Seafarers, Sailors' Society) with contact details distributed to every stranded vessel by May 31, 2026. Evidence of completion: signed welfare plan document, vessel-by-vessel provisions audit, flag-state attaché contact confirmations, and distribution records for welfare organisation contact details.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a crew-relief plan and evacuation coordination; this adaptation anchors the 90-day rotation threshold to the confirmed February 28 blockade start date (making specific seafarers immediately overdue), incorporates the reported US six-month mine-clearance timeline as the planning horizon for provisioning, and names specific maritime welfare organisations rather than leaving support mechanisms undefined.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement AIS management protocols — including the option
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Fleet Operations, in consultation with the company's flag-state authority and legal counsel, must issue a standing AIS management instruction to all masters of vessels operating in or transiting toward the Hormuz strait and the IRGC-controlled channel north of Larak Island, specifying the conditions under which AIS transponder suppression is authorised to balance SOLAS tracking obligations against the confirmed targeting risk demonstrated by the IRGC's April 18, 2026 missile attack on the Indian-flagged Sanmar Herald — which was fired upon despite holding prior IRGC clearance. The instruction must define: (a) the geographic trigger zone for heightened AIS discipline, referenced to the Larak Island channel coordinates; (b) the mandatory UKMTO and flag-state pre-notification procedure before any suppression, to preserve SOLAS compliance; and (c) the logging requirement for all AIS status changes with UTC timestamps and master's written justification. Evidence of completion: signed AIS management instruction issued to all affected fleet masters, UKMTO and flag-state notification template on file, and AIS status log protocols activated in the vessel management system.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires AIS management protocols; this adaptation anchors the targeting threat to the confirmed Sanmar Herald attack of April 18, 2026 — which demonstrates that IRGC clearance does not prevent attack — names the Larak Island channel as the specific geographic trigger, and incorporates the UKMTO pre-notification mechanism necessary to maintain SOLAS compliance, which a generic AIS protocol does not address.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Deploy hardened bridge and accommodation spaces on any
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Before any company vessel is ordered to attempt Hormuz transit — whether under UK escort corridor or independently — the Head of Fleet Operations must confirm in writing that the vessel's bridge and crew accommodation spaces meet a defined hardening standard encompassing: ballistic-resistant bridge windows or blast-deflecting shutters; citadel or hardened muster space with independent ventilation, communications, and 72-hour provisions; and external door and hatch reinforcement against armed forced entry consistent with BMP5 anti-piracy guidance, updated for the IRGC drone, missile, and armed-boarding threat profile evidenced by the April 18, 2026 attack on the Sanmar Herald and the rocket attack on CMA CGM Everglade off the Omani coast. The confirmation must be based on a completed vessel-specific physical security checklist signed by the master and reviewed by the company's Designated Person Ashore (DPA), with deficiencies remediated before the transit order is issued. Evidence of completion: signed physical security checklist per vessel, DPA review record, and a remediation log for any deficiencies identified.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires hardened bridge and accommodation deployment; this adaptation specifies the three hardening categories required against the IRGC's confirmed multi-vector threat (missile, drone, armed boarding), pins the threat assessment to both named vessel incidents (Sanmar Herald and CMA CGM Everglade), and places the gate on the DPA sign-off process so that hardening is verified before — not after — a transit order is issued.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Pre-position armed private maritime security contractors on all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: For every company vessel scheduled for Hormuz transit under the UK/coalition escort corridor, the Head of Fleet Operations must pre-position a team of a minimum four licensed armed private maritime security contractors (PMSCs) — compliant with ISO 28007 and the flag state's PMSC approval list — embarked no later than the vessel's entry into the Gulf of Oman, prior to reaching the Hormuz approach waypoint. The PMSC deployment plan must explicitly account for the gap between HMS Dragon's area-defence capabilities (Type 45 air-defence, 'Beehive' autonomous launch system, Wildcat helicopter) and vessel-level close protection, given that the Sanmar Herald was attacked despite holding IRGC clearance and that HMS Dragon's systems are not designed to prevent IRGC small-boat boardings at individual vessel level. PMSC tasking orders must include rules of engagement for armed boarding resistance, communications protocols with the escort mission's maritime coordination authority, and a post-transit incident debrief requirement. Evidence of completion: PMSC contracts and embarkation records per vessel, flag-state approval documentation, and PMSC tasking order on file reviewed by the DPA.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires armed PMSC pre-positioning; this adaptation makes the vessel-level protection gap explicit by naming HMS Dragon's specific capabilities and their limitations against small-boat boarding — the IRGC's confirmed modus operandi — and requires PMSC deployment before the Gulf of Oman entry point rather than at the strait itself, reflecting the Sanmar Herald's experience of being attacked despite formal clearance.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Quantify and disclose the P&L impact of war-risk
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The CFO must ensure that the company's next quarterly earnings communication or, if material, an interim disclosure issued no later than June 13, 2026, includes a quantified P&L impact statement for war-risk insurance unavailability since March 5, 2026, disclosing: the total incremental premium cost incurred where coverage was obtained at reported rates of 3–10% of vessel value versus the pre-closure baseline of under 1%; the total cargo and hull value exposed without coverage during any period where insurance was unavailable at any price; and a forward-looking sensitivity showing P&L impact under continued closure through the reported US CENTCOM six-month mine-clearance timeline. The disclosure must reference the confirmed March 5, 2026 P&I war-risk cancellation date as the triggering event and be reviewed by external auditors before publication. Evidence of completion: draft disclosure section with line-item quantification reviewed by auditors, CFO sign-off, and publication or filing timestamp.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires P&L disclosure of insurance unavailability; this adaptation specifies three distinct disclosure components (incremental premium cost, uninsured exposure quantum, and forward sensitivity to the six-month CENTCOM timeline), pins the disclosure to the confirmed March 5 cancellation date, and imposes a June 13 deadline that bridges the gap between the confirmed May 24, 2026 incident status and a typical quarterly reporting cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Stress-test liquidity runway against a 6-month Hormuz closure
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The CFO must, by June 6, 2026, deliver to the Board Finance Committee a documented liquidity stress-test modelling a full six-month Hormuz closure from the February 28, 2026 blockade date — consistent with the US CENTCOM-reported mine-clearance timeline — using the confirmed Hapag-Lloyd AG benchmark of $60 million per week in direct closure costs as the calibration rate, scaled to the company's own stranded-vessel count and freight revenue profile. The stress test must: (a) calculate the minimum cash runway at the scaled weekly cost rate under zero-revenue and partial-revenue scenarios; (b) identify each debt covenant threshold (interest coverage, leverage, liquidity ratios) at risk of breach within the six-month window; and (c) document lender notification obligations and waiver request timelines for any covenant at risk, with pre-emptive lender outreach initiated by June 10, 2026 where a breach is projected. Evidence of completion: signed stress-test model with Hapag-Lloyd calibration workings, Board Finance Committee presentation deck, and lender notification log or waiver correspondence where applicable.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a six-month closure stress-test and covenant headroom confirmation; this adaptation calibrates the weekly cost rate to the confirmed Hapag-Lloyd $60 million figure — the only publicly confirmed cost benchmark in the incident record — specifies that both zero- and partial-revenue scenarios must be modelled, and requires proactive lender outreach by a fixed date rather than leaving the covenant response reactive.

incidents.headline: US Section 122 Tariff Authority Invalidated — Global Trade Regime in Flux
incidents.summary: On May 7, 2026, the US Court of International Trade ruled the Trump administration's Section 122 tariff surcharge (a 10% across-the-board import surcharge imposed on nearly all countries after the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs on February 20, 2026) to be unlawful; the decision is under appeal and duties continue to be collected pending outcome, but the authority lapses by statutory deadline on July 24, 2026 unless Congress acts. The administration is simultaneously reconstructing the tariff wall through expanded Section 301 investigations targeting structural manufacturing overcapacity across 16 countries and forced-labour enforcement practices across 60 economies, covering nearly all US imports. This dual legal jeopardy — a CIT ruling against Section 122 and the approaching expiry date — creates acute trade-policy uncertainty for all US import-dependent sectors heading into the in-window reporting period.

secondary_mappings.why: A federal court ruling invalidating the statutory basis for the current tariff surcharge creates an imminent regulatory change event with uncertain implementation timeline for importers.

Legal uncertainty over whether the 10% surcharge will persist, be refunded, or be replaced by higher Section 301 duties directly disrupts import planning and supply-chain cost modelling for all sectors.
Companies holding unliquidated entries subject to contested IEEPA and Section 122 duties face material contingent liabilities and potential refund credits requiring disclosure.
The shift from IEEPA/Section 122 to Section 301/232 restructures the entire US tariff architecture, forcing firms to revise multi-year supply chain and sourcing strategies.

incidents.severity_rationale: Court ruling against the only operative universal tariff authority, combined with a hard statutory expiry on July 24 and active Section 301 investigations covering nearly all US imports, constitutes a high-severity macro trade shock with systemic exposure across all import-dependent sectors.

sources.title: Trump Tariff Tracker — Atlantic Council
The Impact of Trump's Tariffs: A Comprehensive Analysis
Trump 2.0 Tariff Tracker

adaptive_objectives.statement: Monitor and assess the operational and financial impact of acute geopolitical trade-policy shifts — specifically the dual Section 122 legal jeopardy and July 24, 2026 statutory lapse — on the organisation's US import-dependent operations.

Ensure regulatory obligations under active tariff authorities — Section 122 (contested), Section 301 (pending), and prior IEEPA (struck down) — are tracked and that duty payment, protest, and refund obligations are met with legal precision.

Maintain continuous monitoring of the CAFC appellate docket and the USTR Section 301 investigation timeline to ensure the organisation can respond to regulatory change before the July 24, 2026 Section 122 statutory deadline.

Maintain operational continuity of US customs clearance and landed-cost management workflows under conditions of rapidly changing tariff authority, including concurrent Section 122 appellate proceedings and Section 301 investigation conclusions.

Ensure accurate financial reporting of tariff-cost exposures, contingent duty refund assets, and forward-pricing assumptions under Section 122 appeal uncertainty and potential Section 301 successor tariff imposition before July 24, 2026.

Develop and maintain strategic trade-policy contingency plans that address the three distinct outcome paths: Section 122 appellate reversal, Section 122 lapse without replacement, and Section 301 successor tariff activation.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Map every active US import entry to the applicable duty authority — Section 122, Section 301, Section 232, or antidumping/CVD — to isolate which entries are subject to the contested Proclamation 11012 surcharge and eligible for refund protest if the CAFC upholds the CIT's May 7, 2026 ruling.

adaptive_master_controls.adaptive_requirement: File or preserve CBP protests for all Section 122 duties paid under Proclamation 11012 effective February 24, 2026, within applicable protest deadlines, to retain refund eligibility if the CAFC upholds the CIT invalidation after the July 24 lapse.
adaptive_master_controls.statement: File or preserve CBP protests for all Section 122 duties paid under Proclamation 11012 effective February 24, 2026, within applicable protest deadlines, to retain refund eligibility if the CAFC upholds the CIT invalidation after the July 24 lapse.

adaptive_master_controls.adaptive_requirement: Brief the board and external trade counsel on the Oregon v. United States CIT ruling (Slip Op. 26-47) and CAFC administrative stay order of May 12, 2026 to ensure legal obligations and refund-recovery rights are understood at governance level.
adaptive_master_controls.statement: Brief the board and external trade counsel on the Oregon v. United States CIT ruling (Slip Op. 26-47) and CAFC administrative stay order of May 12, 2026 to ensure legal obligations and refund-recovery rights are understood at governance level.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Update the organisation's import compliance manual to reflect the post-February 24, 2026 dual tariff-authority environment — distinguishing Section 122, Section 301, and Section 232 obligations — and flag the July 24, 2026 lapse date as a mandatory review trigger.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Stress-test customs clearance and duty-payment operational workflows for a scenario in which Section 122 authority lapses on July 24, 2026 with no successor tariff in place, requiring immediate re-rating of landed costs across all active US import lanes.

historical_analogues.event_name: Section 232 Steel & Aluminum Tariff Legal Challenges (2018-2020)
historical_analogues.entity: American Institute for International Steel v. United States
historical_analogues.parallel: CAFC ultimately upheld executive Section 232 authority after CIT challenge, setting precedent for broad judicial deference to trade-statute invocation.
historical_analogues.outcome: Section 232 tariffs survived; importers who paid without filing protests lost refund eligibility, validating protest-filing discipline.

historical_analogues.event_name: Nixon Import Surcharge under Trading with the Enemy Act (1971)
historical_analogues.parallel: First and only prior use of comparable emergency import surcharge authority; imposed 10% across-the-board surcharge for 90 days to address balance-of-payments pressure before Bretton Woods collapse.
historical_analogues.outcome: Surcharge lifted after Smithsonian Agreement in December 1971; provided the legislative precedent upon which Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 was later drafted.

graded_claims.claim: On May 7, 2026, the US Court of International Trade issued a 2-1 ruling that the Trump administration's 10% Section 122 global tariff surcharge was unlawful.
The Section 122 tariff was imposed via Presidential Proclamation 11012 on February 20, 2026, effective February 24, 2026, immediately after the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA-based tariffs.
The CIT's injunction is limited to named plaintiffs — Burlap & Barrel, Inc., Basic Fun, Inc., and the State of Washington — no universal injunction was granted.
On May 12, 2026, the US Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit entered an administrative stay, suspending the CIT's order while the appeal proceeds; duties continue to be collected.
Section 122 authority lapses by statutory deadline of July 24, 2026 (150 days from imposition), unless Congress extends it.

The administration is pursuing expanded Section 301 investigations targeting structural manufacturing overcapacity and forced-labour practices, intended to conclude before the Section 122 expiry on July 24, 2026.
Importers who passed through tariff costs may face downstream claims from customers or counterparties seeking reimbursement if refunds are eventually obtained.
Section 301 tariffs replacing Section 122 could approximate the scale and scope of previous IEEPA tariffs with no duration limit.
