adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Disaggregate the 7
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Head of Employee Relations must, before any redundancy notices are issued in any of the five affected hub jurisdictions, produce a jurisdiction-disaggregated notification matrix covering: UK (Collective Redundancies Regulations 1995 — 45-day minimum consultation for 100+ redundancies at a single establishment, with written notice to the Secretary of State via HR1 form); India (Industrial Disputes Act 1947 — 90-day government approval requirement for establishments exceeding 100 workers); Singapore (MOM Tripartite Advisory on Managing Excess Manpower — notification to MOM and the relevant trade union prior to retrenchment); and Poland (EU Collective Redundancies Directive 98/59/EC as implemented — 30-day consultation with employee representatives and notification to the Powiatowy Urząd Pracy). The matrix must specify the earliest legally permissible execution date per jurisdiction for each of the phased tranches running to 2030, must identify whether the 7,800 aggregate reduction triggers thresholds at the establishment level in each country (not merely the group level), and must be signed off by local employment counsel in each jurisdiction and presented to the Standard Chartered Group General Counsel no later than 60 days before the first affected tranche begins. Evidence of completion is the executed matrix with local-counsel sign-off and a Board Audit & Risk Committee confirmation minute.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control sets a general disaggregation obligation; this adaptation specifies the precise statutory instrument, threshold test, and lead time for each of the five named hub jurisdictions implicated by Standard Chartered's announced 7,800-role reduction, and ties completion to the pre-tranche timeline rather than a generic deadline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Produce a skills-gap analysis mapping which of the 7
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief People Officer, in collaboration with the Chief Risk Officer and Chief Compliance Officer, must commission a role-level skills-gap analysis across the reported HR, risk, and compliance functions within the 7,800 elimination scope, specifically identifying tasks that involve non-automatable regulatory judgement — including but not limited to: conduct-risk escalation decisions, sanctions-screening override authorisations, and FCA/MAS supervisory correspondence requiring human attestation — and classifying each such task against the AI tooling proposed to replace the parent role. For every gap where no AI system has been validated to perform the task to the required regulatory standard, a named human-coverage plan must be documented that assigns residual accountability to a specific post-holder, including interim contractor or secondment arrangements, before the phase-one execution date (reported as 2027). The completed analysis, including the coverage plans, must be reviewed by the Board Risk Committee and submitted to relevant regulators (FCA in the UK, MAS in Singapore, SFC in Hong Kong) as a supporting annex to any required regulatory notification of material operational change, with evidence of completion being the signed Board Risk Committee minute and documented regulator submissions.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a generic skills-gap exercise; this adaptation anchors it to the specific function types (HR, risk, compliance) and regulatory-judgement task categories that are material given Standard Chartered's regulated status across five jurisdictions, and ties the output to pre-phase-one regulatory notification requirements.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a transition-support programme for the 7
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief People Officer must design and fund a structured transition-support programme for the approximately 7,800 employees in the affected corporate-function roles, with the programme comprising: (a) an individual skills-assessment for each affected employee completed within 90 days of their role being formally designated for elimination; (b) reskilling pathways toward AI-augmented roles within the bank — with priority placement offers for roles in AI model oversight, data governance, and regulatory-technology operations — or, where internal redeployment is not feasible, externally accredited digital-skills training funded by Standard Chartered; and (c) jurisdiction-specific support packages calibrated to local market conditions in London, Singapore, Hong Kong, India, and Poland. Semi-annual progress reports — covering enrolment rates, internal redeployment numbers, external placement rates, and programme spend — must be presented by the Chief People Officer to the Standard Chartered Remuneration Committee (RemCo) at each scheduled RemCo meeting through 2030, with the first report due no later than six months after the phase-one tranche commences. Evidence of completion is the set of signed RemCo minutes confirming receipt and review of each semi-annual report.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control sets a generic reskilling-and-reporting obligation; this adaptation defines the programme's concrete components, ties the reskilling pathways to the specific function types being replaced by AI, and specifies the RemCo reporting cadence against Standard Chartered's confirmed 2030 timeline.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review and update Standard Chartered's FCA SM&CR accountability
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's UK Chief Compliance Officer must initiate an immediate review of the FCA SM&CR accountability map for all London-based Senior Manager Functions (SMFs) whose supporting human infrastructure falls within the reported scope of the 7,800 role reduction — with particular focus on CF-10 (Compliance Oversight) and CF-28 (Risk) — to confirm that each named SMF holder retains a documented, adequately staffed team capable of discharging their individual regulatory obligations as AI tooling progressively replaces the roles beneath them. The review must produce a line-by-line accountability gap register, updated before each phased headcount-reduction tranche in London, showing: the SMF holder's name and function, the number of supporting roles eliminated to date, the AI system(s) substituting those roles, whether those AI systems have been validated for regulatory-decision use, and the minimum residual human headcount required to keep the SMF holder's accountability defensible to the FCA. The gap register must be approved by the Standard Chartered Group General Counsel, submitted to the FCA as a proactive supervisory notification if any gap is identified, and retained as evidence of SM&CR compliance. Evidence of completion is the signed gap register and, where applicable, the FCA notification log.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control states a general obligation to keep SM&CR maps current; this adaptation names the specific London-based Senior Manager Functions at risk from the reported compliance and risk hub reductions, requires line-by-line tracking against each AI substitution tranche, and triggers proactive FCA notification where gaps are found.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a labour-relations risk assessment covering the UK
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Head of Employee Relations must, treating the May 19, 2026 investor-day announcement as the triggering event, immediately commission a formal labour-relations risk assessment covering the four primary affected jurisdictions — UK, Singapore, India, and Poland — with the assessment to address: (a) UK — whether recognised trade union or employee representative bodies have been or must be notified under the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 and whether the investor-day public announcement preceded any required consultation, creating legal exposure; (b) Singapore — compliance with MOM's Tripartite Advisory notification requirements and the status of any retrenchment notification to the relevant union or employee representative; (c) India — whether state-level Industrial Disputes Act government approval obligations are triggered by the scale of reductions in the India hub, and whether the public announcement pre-empted that process; (d) Poland — whether works-council consultation obligations under the EU Collective Redundancies Directive were satisfied before the May 19 announcement. The completed assessment must be reviewed by local employment counsel in each jurisdiction, escalated to the Group General Counsel within 30 days of the investor-day announcement, and any identified legal exposure must be remediated before further public statements or employee communications are issued in the affected jurisdiction. Evidence of completion is the four-jurisdiction assessment with counsel sign-off and the Group General Counsel's escalation acknowledgement.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control sets a pre-announcement obligation; because the May 19, 2026 investor-day announcement has already occurred, this adaptation reorients the assessment as an immediate post-announcement exposure audit across the four named jurisdictions, focusing on whether the public disclosure preceded legally required consultation processes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Stress-test operational resilience in the compliance and risk
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief Operating Officer, in conjunction with the Chief Risk Officer, must commission a documented operational-resilience stress test for the compliance and risk hubs in Singapore and Hong Kong, modelling a scenario in which the AI tooling deployed to replace eliminated roles underperforms materially — defined as being unable to process at least 80% of the task volume previously handled by human staff — and 20% of the eliminated headcount is simultaneously unavailable to rehire within a 90-day recovery window due to redeployment to competitors or skills atrophy. The stress test must quantify the impact on critical services including: sanctions-screening throughput, regulatory-reporting deadlines (MAS, SFC, and HKMA submission cycles), and conduct-risk escalation response times. For each service where the stress scenario produces a gap exceeding the bank's published Recovery Time Objective, the test must specify a documented contingency response — such as cross-hub reallocation, interim staff augmentation, or temporary manual-processing protocols — with a named accountable executive and an activation trigger. The stress-test results and contingency plans must be approved by the Board Risk Committee before the first reduction tranche affecting the Singapore and Hong Kong hubs is executed, with evidence of completion being the signed Board Risk Committee minute.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a generic resilience test; this adaptation names the specific hub jurisdictions, the specific regulatory reporting cycles and supervisory bodies at risk, and defines the underperformance threshold and rehire-unavailability scenario in quantitative terms tied to Standard Chartered's confirmed operational footprint.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Validate that the AI-investment capex plan underpinning the 7
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief Strategy Officer must present to the Board, no later than 90 days after the May 19, 2026 investor-day announcement, a Board-approved AI-investment dependency schedule that maps every planned headcount-reduction tranche within the 7,800-role programme against the deployment and validation milestones of the specific AI systems designated to replace those roles, demonstrating that in each instance the relevant AI tooling is fully deployed, operationally validated, and regulatory-approved in the applicable jurisdiction before — not after or concurrent with — the tranche's execution date. The schedule must flag any tranche where the AI deployment milestone is currently projected to trail the headcount-reduction date, treat each such instance as a programme hold pending resolution, and require CFO co-signature on any waiver that permits a tranche to proceed before its corresponding AI milestone is met. The Board-approved schedule, including all holds and waivers, must be reported to the Board Risk Committee at each quarterly meeting through 2030. Evidence of completion is the initial Board-approved schedule and the quarterly Board Risk Committee reporting minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a dependency schedule in general terms; this adaptation specifies the sequencing rule — AI deployment before headcount reduction, not concurrent — names the waiver mechanism and CFO co-signature requirement, and ties the cadence to Standard Chartered's confirmed 2030 programme horizon.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Require the Standard Chartered Group CEO and CFO
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group CEO and Group CFO must jointly present to the Board, within 60 days of the May 19, 2026 investor-day announcement, a formal Board-level risk appetite statement specifically addressing the concentration risk created by replacing more than 15% of the support-services workforce — approximately 7,800 roles — with AI systems that have not yet been fully deployed or validated across the bank's operations in Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. The statement must quantify the maximum tolerable proportion of any single corporate function (HR, risk, or compliance) that may be AI-dependent at any point in the programme before human-fallback capacity is confirmed, set explicit circuit-breaker thresholds that halt further reductions if AI-system performance falls below defined operational benchmarks, and address the reputational and regulatory risk of pursuing this transformation from a record $2.5 billion pre-tax profit position — which removes the financial-stress justification for the scale and speed of the workforce reduction. The Board must vote to adopt or reject the statement as a formal risk-appetite instrument, and any subsequent deviation from its thresholds must be escalated to the full Board for approval. Evidence of completion is the signed Board resolution adopting the risk appetite statement.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a generic board risk appetite statement; this adaptation anchors it to the specific concentration metric (>15% of support-services workforce), names the three function categories at risk, sets the expectation of circuit-breaker thresholds, and highlights that the record-profit context removes the financial-necessity framing that might otherwise justify urgency.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Ensure that the May 19 2026 investor-day disclosure
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group General Counsel and Group Chief Communications Officer must jointly commission an immediate legal and reputational review of the specific language used in the May 19, 2026 investor-day materials — in particular the characterisation of eliminated roles as 'lower-value human capital' — to assess: (a) employment-law exposure in the UK (Equality Act 2010 and unfair dismissal risk where the framing could be characterised as pre-determining individual worth), Singapore (Employment Act and Tripartite Guidelines on Fair Employment Practices), India (applicable labour law and constitutional dignity provisions), and Poland (EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 31 and national labour code worker-dignity provisions); and (b) reputational exposure including regulatory supervisory risk where the FCA, MAS, SFC, or HKMA may view the framing as inconsistent with the bank's obligations to treat employees with dignity and maintain a sound culture. The review must produce a written legal-risk opinion and a revised communications lexicon approved by the Group General Counsel, which must replace the 'lower-value human capital' framing in all subsequent regulatory filings, investor presentations, and employee communications before any such document is published. Evidence of completion is the signed legal-risk opinion and the approved revised-lexicon document, with a log of all materials amended.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control sets a general obligation to review disclosure language; this adaptation names the precise phrase at issue, identifies the specific statutes and supervisory bodies in each affected jurisdiction that create legal and regulatory exposure, and requires a concrete replacement lexicon rather than a generic review finding.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Document the AI systems intended to replace the HR
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief Technology Officer and Chief Data Officer must produce, within 90 days of the May 19, 2026 investor-day announcement and before any role elimination tranche is executed, a comprehensive AI-system inventory for every automated tool or platform designated to replace functions within the 7,800-role scope in HR, risk, and compliance, documenting for each system: (a) the specific task categories it is intended to perform and the roles it replaces; (b) current model-validation status — including whether independent validation has been completed and the date of the most recent validation report; (c) regulatory-approval status in each of the five named hub jurisdictions (London: FCA; Singapore: MAS; Hong Kong: SFC/HKMA; India: RBI where applicable; Poland: KNF and applicable EU AI Act requirements); and (d) documented fallback human-in-the-loop procedures specifying who performs the task manually, to what standard, and within what timeframe if the AI system is unavailable or underperforms. The inventory must be reviewed and approved by the Chief Risk Officer and Chief Compliance Officer and submitted to the Board Risk Committee before the first tranche begins. Evidence of completion is the signed inventory document and the Board Risk Committee approval minute.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a generic AI-system documentation obligation; this adaptation names the five specific hub jurisdictions with their respective regulators, ties the documentation to the elimination-tranche sequencing, and requires jurisdiction-specific regulatory-approval status rather than a single consolidated assessment.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Apply Standard Chartered's model risk management framework —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief Model Risk Officer must ensure that every AI model designated to substitute a previously human-held compliance or risk-decision function within the 7,800-role programme — including models replacing sanctions-screening review, regulatory-reporting preparation, conduct-risk escalation assessment, and credit-risk support functions — is subject to a full model-risk-management validation cycle under Standard Chartered's internal MRM framework, incorporating SR 11-7 principles for any models booked to or governing US-linked entities or portfolios, before the role that model replaces is eliminated. The validation must include: independent third-party or second-line review of model conceptual soundness; back-testing against historical human-decision outcomes; documentation of known model limitations; and approval by the Model Risk Committee with a formal use-approval certificate. No headcount-reduction decision affecting a role where a substituting model has not received a use-approval certificate may proceed, and the Chief Model Risk Officer must maintain a live register of certificate status for all in-scope models, reported to the Board Risk Committee quarterly. Evidence of completion is the use-approval certificate register and the quarterly Board Risk Committee reporting minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control sets a general SR 11-7 application obligation; this adaptation names the specific compliance and risk decision-types at stake within Standard Chartered's announced programme, requires a use-approval certificate as the hard gate before role elimination, and specifies SR 11-7 applicability specifically to US-booked entities rather than applying it universally.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a vendor concentration review for any single
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief Procurement Officer and Chief Technology Officer must, within 60 days of the May 19, 2026 investor-day announcement, conduct a vendor concentration review covering every AI platform contracted or under consideration to replace back-office functions within the 7,800-role scope across the five named hub jurisdictions — London, Singapore, Hong Kong, India, and Poland — to identify instances where a single vendor or platform is designated to replace functions in two or more corporate function categories (HR, risk, and compliance) simultaneously or across two or more jurisdictions. For any single-vendor exposure identified, the review must assess whether that concentration exceeds the bank's third-party concentration risk appetite, document the systemic dependency created if that vendor experiences an outage, regulatory sanction, or contractual failure, and either (a) produce a Board Risk Committee-approved exception with documented mitigation (including a named alternative-vendor contingency) or (b) require the programme plan to be restructured to distribute the relevant functions across multiple vendors before the affected tranche executes. The completed review and any exceptions or restructuring decisions must be approved by the Chief Risk Officer and reported to the Board Risk Committee before the first multi-function tranche is executed. Evidence of completion is the signed vendor-concentration register and the Board Risk Committee approval minute.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The master control requires a generic vendor concentration review; this adaptation identifies Standard Chartered's specific risk vector — a single AI platform replacing multiple function categories across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously — names the three corporate function types in scope, and requires either a mitigation plan or programme restructuring as a hard gate rather than a monitoring recommendation.

incidents.headline: LIRR 3-day strike ends; MTA board approves worker refunds
incidents.summary: Five unions representing approximately 3,500 Long Island Rail Road workers — including locomotive engineers, signalmen, machinists, electricians, and communications workers — launched a strike at 12:01 a.m. on May 16, 2026, the first LIRR strike in over 30 years, shutting down the busiest commuter rail system in North America and stranding roughly 250,000 daily weekday riders. A tentative agreement was announced on May 18 (Monday) and service resumed May 19 at noon, with the deal providing annual raises of 3%, 3%, 3.5%, and 4.5% plus a $3,000 lump-sum payment and full retroactive pay. On May 20, 2026, the MTA Board approved an approximately 13% prorated refund to May monthly ticket holders for the four days of disruption, with ongoing refund processing and contract ratification proceedings still active within the sweep window.

secondary_mappings.why: Complete service suspension for 3+ days across North America's largest commuter rail system constitutes a major operational continuity failure affecting 250,000 daily riders.

State and federal government intervention (Gov. Hochul, Presidential Emergency Board), widespread public disruption, and sustained media coverage created significant stakeholder and reputational pressure on MTA leadership.

Dispute resolution was governed by the 1926 Railway Labor Act, National Mediation Board, and two Presidential Emergency Boards, placing the labour action within a multi-agency federal regulatory framework.

incidents.severity_rationale: First LIRR strike in 32 years; 3,500 workers across 5 unions walked out, halting service for 250,000 daily commuters for three days, triggering Governor and Presidential Emergency Board intervention and MTA board refund action.

sources.title: LIRR strike update: Unions, MTA reach agreement to end 3-day walkout
What to Know About the Long Island Rail Road Strike
LIRR service has resumed

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure that labour relations governance, collective bargaining readiness, and strike contingency protocols are maintained so that workforce disputes do not cause unplanned service cessation or material financial obligations.
Maintain operational continuity plans capable of sustaining essential commuter rail service or credible alternative transit coverage during a total workforce stoppage.
Ensure service-resumption procedures — including safety inspection, crew recall, and phased schedule restoration — are documented and executable within a defined recovery window.

Protect organisational reputation with key stakeholders — including daily riders, elected officials, and bondholders — through proactive, transparent communication during and after a service disruption.

graded_claims.claim: Five unions representing approximately 3,500 LIRR workers launched a strike at 12:01 a.m. on May 16, 2026
The strike was the first LIRR work stoppage in over 30 years
The LIRR is the busiest commuter rail system in North America serving roughly 250,000 daily weekday riders
A tentative agreement was announced on May 18, 2026 and service resumed May 19 at noon
The deal provides annual raises of 3%, 3%, 3.5%, and 4.5% plus a $3,000 lump-sum payment and full retroactive pay
On May 20, 2026, the MTA Board approved an approximately 13% prorated refund to May monthly ticket holders for the four days of disruption
Metro-North unions are expected to seek wage parity with LIRR workers following the settlement
Contract ratification proceedings for the five LIRR unions remain active

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain a documented multi-union bargaining calendar that tracks
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Chief Labor Relations Officer must maintain a live, board-accessible CBA calendar itemising the individual expiry dates for each of the five LIRR unions (locomotive engineers, signalmen, machinists, electricians, and communications workers) that ratified the May 2026 tentative agreement, with automated 18-month and 6-month advance alerts tied to each expiry. The calendar must embed the May 2026 wage benchmarks — 3%, 3%, 3.5%, and 4.5% annual raises plus a $3,000 lump-sum — as a pattern-wage reference floor, flagging any subsequent MTA-system negotiation in which a union seeks materially different terms so that concurrent or clustered expiry risk can be escalated to the MTA Board Labor Committee before bargaining opens. Completion is evidenced by a signed calendar document published to the MTA Board portal within 60 days of final ratification of all five LIRR CBAs, with alert-trigger logic validated by the MTA's enterprise risk management team.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses multi-union calendar hygiene broadly; this adaptation anchors the obligation specifically to the five named LIRR craft unions whose simultaneous strike on May 16, 2026 produced the first LIRR work stoppage in over 30 years, and locks the May 2026 wage ramp as the explicit benchmark floor to detect pattern-bargaining pressure before the next expiry cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a standing strike-contingency workforce plan calibrated to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The LIRR President, in coordination with the MTA Chief Operating Officer, must produce and annually exercise a written strike-contingency workforce plan dimensioned to the confirmed five-union, 3,500-worker LIRR labour structure, specifying the sequential recall order for each of the five crafts (locomotive engineers, signalmen, machinists, electricians, and communications workers) and the minimum staffing thresholds — by craft and by corridor — required to operate safe revenue service across the LIRR network. The plan must define a 72-hour resumption clock that begins at tentative-agreement announcement, consistent with the May 18-to-May 19 noon actual resumption interval, and must identify which craft's recall is the critical-path dependency for FRA-compliant service restart. Evidence of completion is a signed, dated plan document reviewed by the MTA Chief Safety Officer, plus a tabletop exercise completed within 12 months, with a written after-action report submitted to the MTA Board Operations Committee.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control establishes a general strike-contingency workforce plan; this adaptation is calibrated to the specific five-craft union structure and the demonstrated 72-hour resolution-to-resumption window proved achievable in the May 16-19, 2026 stoppage, replacing abstract thresholds with corridor-level and craft-level staffing floors grounded in the actual LIRR network.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate and post-incident review the LIRR bus-bridge and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The LIRR Vice President of Operations must conduct and document a structured post-incident review of the bus-bridge and NICE Bus surge-capacity deployment activated during the May 16-19, 2026 LIRR strike, assessing, for each of the four disruption days, which service-level commitments for the approximately 250,000 affected daily weekday riders were met, partially met, or missed, with quantified ridership throughput data per route corridor. The review must identify any pre-positioning gaps — including NICE Bus fleet availability ceilings, driver hour limitations, and terminal capacity constraints at Penn Station and Atlantic Terminal — and produce a binding remediation plan specifying pre-negotiated surge-capacity agreements with NICE Bus and New York City Transit that can be activated within four hours of a strike commencement. The completed review and remediation plan, signed by the LIRR President, must be submitted to the MTA Board Operations Committee no later than 90 days after May 20, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for activation and review of bus-bridge contingencies; this adaptation requires quantified, corridor-level measurement of actual throughput against commitments for the confirmed 250,000-rider daily baseline during the specific four-day May 2026 outage, converting the review into an enforceable agreement with named third-party operators.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define and test a service-resumption checklist for the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The LIRR Chief Engineer and Chief Mechanical Officer must jointly develop, document, and test a network-wide service-resumption checklist that replicates the operational conditions of the May 19, 2026 noon restart, covering FRA-required track-geometry clearance inspections for all mainline and branch segments, end-to-end signal-system verification across the LIRR's interlocking network, and rolling-stock pre-positioning at all originating yards to support first-revenue-service departures. The checklist must be sequenced so that all prerequisite tasks — beginning with signal-system verification as the confirmed long-lead item — can be completed within 24 hours of a formal strike-resolution notice, and must assign a named role accountable for each task with a sign-off timestamp field. Completion is evidenced by a fully executed dry-run of the checklist against the May 19 resumption timeline, with variance analysis submitted to the MTA Chief Safety Officer and MTA Board Operations Committee within 60 days of final CBA ratification.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control specifies a service-resumption checklist for rail networks; this adaptation is explicitly sequenced to the confirmed 24-hour window between the May 18 tentative-agreement announcement and the May 19 noon revenue-service restart, embedding FRA-specific inspection steps and LIRR-specific yard pre-positioning requirements as named checklist items rather than generic categories.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Quantify the revenue and ridership loss from the May 16-19
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Chief Financial Officer must commission and publish a quantified financial-impact baseline for the May 16-19, 2026 LIRR four-day stoppage, incorporating: confirmed farebox revenue loss across the four disruption days calculated against the approximately 250,000 daily weekday rider baseline; the MTA Board-approved approximately 13% prorated refund liability for May 2026 monthly-ticket holders approved on May 20, 2026; and incremental operating costs from the bus-bridge deployment. This baseline scenario must be formally incorporated into the MTA's business-continuity financial model as the reference case for a four-day LIRR outage, with sensitivity analyses extending the scenario to 7-day and 14-day durations to bracket future risk. The completed model, reviewed by the MTA's independent financial advisor, must be presented to the MTA Finance Committee within 90 days of May 20, 2026, with the refund-processing reconciliation included once ticket-holder refunds are fully disbursed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires revenue and ridership loss quantification for business-continuity modelling; this adaptation anchors the model to the confirmed four-day LIRR outage and the specific MTA Board-approved approximately 13% refund liability as the verified cost floor, then requires duration-scaled sensitivity analysis that the incident itself did not necessitate but a recurrence could.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review LIRR critical-path dependencies — specifically Penn Station
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Vice President of Network Planning, in coordination with Amtrak and NJ Transit, must conduct a documented critical-path dependency analysis of LIRR operations at Penn Station and Atlantic Terminal, determining the slot-allocation and throughput thresholds at which an LIRR service suspension longer than the confirmed three-day May 16-19, 2026 window would trigger cascading restrictions on Amtrak Northeast Corridor trains and NJ Transit services sharing those facilities. The analysis must define quantitative trip-count and headway-impact triggers — by terminal and by time-of-peak — and establish pre-agreed inter-agency communication protocols with Amtrak and NJ Transit operations centers specifying escalation actions if those thresholds are approached. A signed analysis report and a joint inter-agency protocol document must be completed within 120 days of May 20, 2026, and reviewed annually or upon any material change to Penn Station slot assignments.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires review of critical-path infrastructure dependencies; this adaptation names Penn Station and Atlantic Terminal as the confirmed shared facilities, uses the three-day May 2026 stoppage as the known baseline that did not trigger cascading restrictions, and requires the analysis to define the precise threshold beyond which Amtrak and NJ Transit throughput would be materially impaired — turning a retrospective review into a forward-looking trigger framework.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Document stakeholder communication protocols tested during the May
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Chief Communications Officer must produce a structured after-action documentation package capturing all stakeholder communication actions taken during the May 16-19, 2026 LIRR strike, including: the timing, channel, and content of each rider notification issued from strike commencement at 12:01 a.m. on May 16 through service resumption on May 19 at noon; the internal briefing sequence and timing for elected officials at city, state, and federal levels; and the MTA Board communication cadence that preceded the May 20, 2026 refund-approval vote. The package must identify gaps in notification cadence or channel coverage — specifically any periods exceeding two hours without a public rider update during the four disruption days — and convert the documented protocols into a replicable template with pre-assigned roles, pre-drafted message skeletons, and defined escalation timelines for future labour disruptions. The completed template must be approved by the MTA President and distributed to all MTA communications and government-affairs leads within 75 days of May 20, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires documentation of stakeholder communication protocols tested during the incident; this adaptation reconstructs the specific notification timeline from the confirmed May 16, 2026 12:01 a.m. strike start through the May 20 board vote, explicitly calling out the rider-notification cadence gap as the identified failure mode to be corrected in the replicable template.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Prepare a post-incident stakeholder communication package addressing the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Chief Communications Officer, in coordination with the MTA CFO, must publish and distribute a stakeholder communication package within 10 business days of the May 20, 2026 MTA Board refund-approval vote, addressed to all May 2026 LIRR monthly-ticket holders, that confirms: the approximately 13% prorated refund amount approved for the four days of service disruption (May 16-19); the precise processing timeline and expected credit or payment date by ticket-purchase channel (web, app, and station); and the eligibility criteria and dispute-resolution contact for ticket holders who believe their refund was miscalculated or not received. The package must also include a one-page factual summary of the strike timeline and resolution for distribution to elected officials and media to pre-empt reputational escalation around refund delays. Completion is evidenced by distribution records confirming outreach to all eligible ticket holders, a published FAQ on the MTA website, and a sign-off memo from the MTA President.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a post-incident stakeholder communication package for a refund decision; this adaptation specifies the confirmed approximately 13% prorated refund figure approved on May 20, 2026, requires channel-specific processing timelines matched to the LIRR's actual ticket-purchase infrastructure, and adds a proactive elected-official brief to address the reputational risk of visible refund processing delays following a historically significant stoppage.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Develop a media and public-affairs response playbook anchored
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Chief Communications Officer must develop and executive-approve a written media and public-affairs response playbook specifically calibrated to an LIRR labour stoppage, incorporating pre-approved message templates for three confirmed critical communication moments: strike day-1 (covering immediate service suspension, the approximately 250,000-rider impact, and alternative-transport activation); strike day-3 (covering continuation or near-resolution status, sustained rider impact, and elected-official coordination); and settlement announcement (covering tentative-agreement confirmation, service-resumption timing, and ticket-refund commitment). Each template must designate a named MTA spokesperson, specify approved distribution channels and maximum time-to-publish from trigger event, and include a statement that acknowledges the LIRR's status as North America's busiest commuter railroad to contextualise public impact without amplifying alarm. The playbook must be reviewed by the MTA President and MTA General Counsel, finalised within 90 days of May 20, 2026, and rehearsed in a tabletop communications exercise within 12 months.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a media response playbook referencing the LIRR's scale; this adaptation structures the playbook around the three specific confirmed communication inflection points from the May 16-18, 2026 strike period — day-1, day-3, and settlement — treating the observed narrative gaps during those days as the explicit design requirement for the pre-approved message templates.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Brief MTA bondholders and rating agencies within 30
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The MTA Chief Financial Officer, supported by the MTA's investor-relations counsel, must deliver a written briefing to MTA bondholders and all three major rating agencies no later than 30 days after final ratification of the five LIRR union contracts, quantifying: the incremental annual labour cost obligation arising from the confirmed wage ramp (3%, 3%, 3.5%, and 4.5% across the agreement term plus the $3,000 lump-sum and full retroactive pay) applied to the approximately 3,500-worker LIRR workforce; the one-time financial impact of the MTA Board-approved approximately 13% prorated refund for May 2026 monthly-ticket holders; and the combined effect of both items against the MTA's current debt-service coverage ratios and operating budget projections. The briefing must include a forward-looking statement addressing how the MTA intends to offset incremental labour costs within its existing capital and operating plans, and must be reviewed and approved by the MTA CFO and MTA General Counsel before distribution. Completion is evidenced by confirmed delivery receipts from each rating agency and a signed distribution log retained by the MTA CFO's office.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a bondholder and rating-agency brief within 30 days of settlement; this adaptation anchors the financial disclosure to the confirmed specific wage-ramp percentages and $3,000 lump-sum for the confirmed 3,500-worker LIRR workforce alongside the confirmed approximately 13% refund liability, requiring the MTA to present both items together against actual debt-service coverage metrics rather than in isolation.

incidents.headline: USW ratification vote on Arconic tentative contract set for May 27
incidents.summary: United Steelworkers members across four Arconic facilities — in Massena, NY; Davenport, IA; Alcoa, TN; and Lafayette, IN — voted unanimously on May 14, 2026 to authorise a strike after the existing master contract expired May 16 with no new deal, citing Arconic's concessionary proposals including a 67% healthcare premium increase for single employees and a 255% increase for families. A tentative agreement was announced on May 16 at the contract expiration deadline, providing 5% pay increase in year one, 4% in years two and three, and zero changes to healthcare premiums, co-pays, or deductibles through June 2029. USW Local 420-A members at the Massena facility are scheduled to vote on ratification on May 27, 2026, keeping the labour action in live status within the sweep window.

secondary_mappings.why: A failed ratification vote on May 27 would trigger an immediate strike across four manufacturing sites covering ~3,400 workers, creating a live operational continuity risk within the sweep window.
Healthcare premium increases of up to 255% for families were the primary sticking point, directly implicating worker health coverage and financial wellbeing as a structural dispute driver.
Arconic's concessionary bargaining posture amid industry headwinds from new competitors and shifting demand signals longer-term workforce cost strategy concerns affecting site viability.

incidents.severity_rationale: ~3,400 workers across four US manufacturing sites at risk; ratification vote pending within the window creates a live strike threat; healthcare and seniority provisions at stake affect long-term employee relations at a strategically important aluminium manufacturer.

sources.title: Union workers at Arconic authorize strike as contract nears expiration
Massena's United Steelworkers union members to vote on Arconic deal
Arconic workers to vote on contract that preserves health care benefits

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure collective bargaining obligations under the USW master contract are met through ratification, avoiding an unlawful unilateral change to terms and conditions at Massena, Davenport, Alcoa TN, and Lafayette.

Protect worker health and safety standards by ensuring the finalised USW agreement preserves healthcare premium, co-pay, and deductible levels through June 2029, eliminating the 67% single / 255% family increase risk.
Maintain operational continuity across four Arconic production facilities during the ratification window, preventing unplanned production interruption pending USW Local 420-A Massena vote on May 27.
Preserve supply-chain commitments to aerospace and automotive customers by securing contract continuity before inventory buffers are exhausted at Davenport Works and Lafayette.
Align labour relations strategy with Arconic's post-take-private financial obligations to Apollo, ensuring strike avoidance protects EBITDA margin and refinancing optionality.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must complete a formal ratification-readiness review for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By end of business May 26, 2026, Arconic's Vice President of Labour Relations must complete and sign a ratification-readiness memorandum for USW Local 420-A at Massena, NY, documenting all known member concerns about the tentative agreement terms surfaced between May 16 and May 26 — including any objections to the wage schedule or the healthcare freeze language — and assigning a probability-weighted rejection risk rating (Low / Medium / High) with supporting rationale. The memorandum must be distributed to Arconic's Chief Human Resources Officer, the Massena facility HR lead, and the lead negotiator no later than 06:00 ET on May 27, 2026, prior to the opening of the ratification ballot. Evidence of completion is the signed, dated memorandum with distribution receipts.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a ratification-readiness review; this adaptation pins accountability to a named role, sets the completion deadline to the eve of the confirmed May 27 Massena ballot, and requires an explicit rejection-probability rating grounded in the specific 125-member Local 420-A population rather than a generic workforce census.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must maintain a labour-action contingency roster covering
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Director of Operations Continuity must maintain a live labour-action contingency roster, segmented by facility, that maps essential-operations roles against the confirmed USW membership counts: 125 Local 420-A members at Massena (of 160 total employees), approximately 1,800 Local 105 members at Davenport Works (of ~2,400 workers), and the remaining approximately 1,475 members across Alcoa, TN and Lafayette, IN. The roster must specify, by name or title, which supervisory and management personnel will assume coverage of each critical production function — including furnace operations, casting, and rolling at Davenport — within four hours of a confirmed rejection on May 27, and must designate a cross-facility supervisory coordinator to manage resource reallocation across all four plants simultaneously. The completed roster must be validated and approved by the Senior Vice President of Operations no later than 12:00 ET on May 26, 2026, with a copy held at each facility HR office.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a labour-action contingency roster; this adaptation enforces facility-by-facility segmentation using the confirmed membership numbers, sets a pre-vote validation deadline, and names Davenport's specific production functions — furnace, casting, rolling — as the highest-exposure operations requiring explicit role-by-role supervisory coverage.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must document that the healthcare provision freeze
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's labour relations communications team must confirm, by 17:00 ET on May 26, 2026, that written notice of the tentative agreement's healthcare provision — specifically that premiums, co-pays, and deductibles will remain unchanged through June 2029 — has been distributed to all USW members across the four facilities (Massena, Davenport, Alcoa TN, Lafayette) via a channel producing a retrievable delivery record (e.g., posted certified notice at union bulletin boards, timestamped email to union stewards, or signed acknowledgement log). If the reported healthcare cost-shift proposals — a 67% single-employee and 255% family premium increase — are confirmed as having been on the table, the written notice must explicitly state that those proposals were withdrawn in the final tentative agreement, to preclude a post-ratification claim that members voted without understanding what was rescinded. Evidence of completion is a distribution log showing facility, delivery method, timestamp, and confirming recipient for each location.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires written communication of healthcare terms; this adaptation sets a hard pre-ballot deadline, specifies the four named facilities, requires a retrievable delivery record rather than general 'communication,' and — treating the healthcare cost-shift as a conditional contingency — mandates explicit withdrawal language if those proposals are confirmed, directly addressing the post-vote information-dispute risk unique to this negotiation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic HR must brief all four facility HR
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Chief Human Resources Officer must convene a mandatory synchronisation briefing — in person or via videoconference — with the HR leads at all four USW facilities (Massena, Davenport Works, Alcoa TN, Lafayette) no later than 15:00 ET on May 26, 2026, covering the exact wage schedule (5% in year one, 4% in year two, 4% in year three) and the confirmed zero-change healthcare terms through June 2029, with a scripted Q&A document providing standardised answers to the fifteen most likely worker questions. Each facility HR lead must confirm receipt and readiness in writing before the briefing concludes. Evidence of completion is the signed attendance log from the briefing, the distributed Q&A document, and four written confirmations from facility HR leads.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires HR briefings on agreement terms; this adaptation mandates a single synchronised pre-ballot session with a hard deadline tied to the May 27 Massena vote, requires a standardised Q&A script to prevent facility-to-facility inconsistency — the specific failure mode created by four geographically dispersed plants — and sets a written confirmation requirement from each of the four named facility leads.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must maintain a record of USW negotiating-committee
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's General Counsel must ensure that a complete, sequenced record of all communications between Arconic's negotiating team and the USW negotiating committee from May 14, 2026 (date of the unanimous strike authorisation vote) through May 16, 2026 (date of tentative agreement announcement and contract expiration) is compiled and preserved in a litigation-hold file labelled 'USW 2026 CBA — NLRB Region 3 Privilege Archive' by May 28, 2026. The archive must include, at minimum: all written proposals and counter-proposals exchanged at the table, any bargaining session notes or summaries, and records of any side communications with USW Local officers during the two-day window. The General Counsel must certify in writing that the archive is complete and that document-preservation obligations under NLRB Region 3 procedural rules have been satisfied. Evidence of completion is the signed certification and a file index with document counts.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a record of negotiating communications; this adaptation pins the preservation window to the confirmed May 14–16 strike-authorisation-to-tentative-agreement sequence, designates the specific NLRB jurisdictional region (Region 3, which covers western New York and includes Massena), and requires a named litigation-hold label and General Counsel certification to satisfy the ULP-defence specificity that a generic recordkeeping directive would not achieve.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must activate a 72-hour production-continuity protocol at
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Effective 18:00 ET on May 27, 2026 — contingent on a confirmed rejection vote by USW Local 420-A at Massena — Arconic's Senior Vice President of Operations must activate the 72-hour production-continuity protocol for Davenport Works (USW Local 105, 1,800+ members) and Lafayette (approximately 600 USW members), specifying by name or role the minimum supervisory staffing required to maintain safe hot-line furnace temperatures and prevent rolling-mill equipment damage during any period of reduced or halted union-labour input. The protocol must define discrete go/no-go decision points at the 24-hour and 48-hour marks for escalating from reduced operations to controlled shutdown, and must designate a single Operations Incident Commander with authority to make those calls across both facilities. Evidence of completion is the signed protocol activation record, the staffing schedule for hours 0–72, and written go/no-go criteria signed off by the SVP of Operations.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a production-continuity protocol; this adaptation ties activation to the confirmed May 27 rejection trigger, names Davenport and Lafayette specifically with their USW membership counts, and identifies furnace temperature maintenance and rolling-mill equipment integrity — the irreversible physical failure modes unique to aluminium production — as the minimum-staffing threshold, rather than a generic 'essential operations' formulation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must validate that customer-facing force-majeure or supply-interruption
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Chief Commercial Officer, in coordination with outside counsel, must confirm by 12:00 ET on May 27, 2026 — before ratification results are known — that supply-interruption or force-majeure notice templates have been prepared for all aerospace and automotive customer accounts whose delivery obligations could be affected by a work stoppage at any of the four USW facilities, and that the internal escalation path from a confirmed Local 420-A rejection to authorised customer notification can be executed within 24 hours. The confirmation must specify, for each customer account in scope, the contractual notice provision being invoked, the designated Arconic account manager responsible for delivering notice, and whether notice requires senior officer countersignature. Evidence of completion is a signed readiness checklist listing each customer, the applicable contract clause, the delivery channel, and the authorised signatory, approved by the CCO and General Counsel.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires validation of customer-notice obligations; this adaptation sets a pre-vote deadline so the 24-hour trigger window remains achievable regardless of when results are announced, identifies aerospace and automotive as the specific customer segments at risk given Davenport Works' role in those supply chains, and requires contract-clause-level specificity rather than a general force-majeure reference.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must confirm that finished-goods and work-in-progress inventories
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 17:00 ET on May 26, 2026, Arconic's Vice President of Supply Chain must produce a written inventory-position report for Davenport Works quantifying finished-goods and work-in-progress stock of can-body sheet (against Crown Holdings delivery schedules) and automotive sheet (against Ford F-150 program delivery windows) sufficient to assess coverage under a 14-day strike scenario. The report must state, in days-of-supply terms, the current stock position for each product line, identify the earliest delivery commitment that could not be met if Davenport Works production halted on May 27, and flag any customer order where the coverage gap falls inside the force-majeure notice window. Evidence of completion is the signed inventory-position report reviewed and approved by the SVP of Operations and Chief Commercial Officer before market open on May 27.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires inventory confirmation; this adaptation names Crown Holdings (can-body sheet) and the Ford F-150 program (automotive sheet) as the specific customer dependencies at Davenport Works, requires a days-of-supply quantification against a defined 14-day scenario rather than a binary sufficiency judgment, and ties the report deadline to the pre-ballot window so findings can inform the force-majeure readiness review under AC-OPS-003-LAB-01.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must maintain a strike-specific incident-management cadence —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Chief Operating Officer must establish a strike-specific incident-management cadence beginning at 07:00 ET on May 27, 2026, consisting of: (1) a daily 07:00 ET senior leadership briefing attended by the COO, CHRO, CCO, General Counsel, and all four facility operations leads, covering ratification status, production status, customer-communication status, and any NLRB or regulatory developments; (2) a real-time status dashboard updated no less than every four hours showing union membership engagement status at each of the four facilities; and (3) a defined stand-down trigger — full ratification confirmation across all four USW locals — that formally closes the incident-management cadence. The cadence must remain active until stand-down is confirmed in writing by the COO. Evidence of completion at any point is the meeting attendance log and dashboard snapshot for that day.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a strike-specific incident-management cadence; this adaptation sets a specific start time tied to the May 27 ballot, names the mandatory attendees by role, requires a four-facility real-time dashboard rather than ad hoc reporting, and defines a binary stand-down trigger — full ratification across all four USW locals — that prevents premature closure after only the Massena vote is resolved.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic's board and Apollo Global Management must receive
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Chief Financial Officer must deliver a pre-vote financial exposure briefing to Arconic's board and to Apollo Global Management's portfolio operations team by 09:00 ET on May 27, 2026, quantifying EBITDA impact under a 30-day and 60-day simultaneous work stoppage at all four USW facilities, with Davenport Works modelled separately given its contribution of more than 1,800 of the ~3,400 total USW-represented employees and its concentration of can-body sheet and automotive sheet revenue. The briefing must include lost-revenue estimates by product line, incremental costs of any contingency staffing or production diversion, and an assessment of covenant headroom under Apollo's post-take-private leverage parameters. Evidence of completion is the dated briefing deck with Apollo's written acknowledgement of receipt.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a board and sponsor briefing on work-stoppage EBITDA exposure; this adaptation sets a pre-ballot deadline of May 27 morning so Apollo can inform any post-rejection decisions in real time, requires Davenport Works to be modelled as a standalone line item reflecting its outsized share of the USW workforce and revenue base, and explicitly ties the analysis to Apollo's leverage covenant parameters — the financial constraint unique to Arconic's post-take-private capital structure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must update its three-year labour cost model
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Chief Financial Officer must update the company's three-year labour cost model, incorporating the confirmed 5%-4%-4% wage schedule and the healthcare-terms freeze through June 2029 applicable to all ~3,400 USW members across Massena, Davenport, Alcoa TN, and Lafayette, and must present the revised model to Apollo Global Management's finance team by June 10, 2026. The model update must include a covenant-stress test showing cumulative incremental labour cost in years one through three relative to the 2025 baseline, and must flag any scenario — including full ratification followed by attrition or overtime increases — in which the resulting cost trajectory would compress EBITDA below Apollo's leverage-covenant maintenance threshold. Evidence of completion is the updated model file with version control log and the Apollo finance team's written receipt of the presentation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a labour cost model update; this adaptation uses the confirmed wage and healthcare terms rather than negotiating proposals, applies them to the full confirmed USW membership count across all four named facilities, sets a specific post-ratification deadline, and requires an explicit covenant-stress test against Apollo's leverage parameters — the private-equity ownership constraint that makes this model update strategically material beyond standard HR planning.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Arconic must scenario-plan a repeat negotiation-cycle risk for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Arconic's Senior Vice President of Labour Relations, in coordination with the CFO and Chief Strategy Officer, must initiate a next-cycle risk scenario-planning exercise by August 1, 2026, documenting the structural conditions that produced the May 2026 near-strike — including the healthcare cost-shift dynamic, the four-facility simultaneity of the USW master contract, and the proximity of the contract expiry to key customer delivery cycles — and producing a written assessment of the likelihood and form of a comparable confrontation at the next master contract expiry (approximately May 2029). The assessment must recommend specific bargaining-preparation milestones beginning no later than November 2028, and must be reviewed by Arconic's board compensation and labour committee before year-end 2026. Evidence of completion is the signed scenario assessment and the board committee review minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires next-cycle scenario planning; this adaptation names the specific causal factors from the 2026 near-miss — healthcare cost-shifting, four-plant master-contract simultaneity, and delivery-cycle timing — as the analytical anchors, ties the planning horizon to the confirmed approximate May 2029 expiry, and sets a board-level review deadline before year-end 2026 so findings inform the multi-year Apollo investment horizon rather than remaining an HR planning document.
